uk1BbmQo6Fk.title: Euphoria Season 3 | Behind The Scenes Episode 3 | HBO Max
uk1BbmQo6Fk.synopsis: This is the official HBO Max behind-the-scenes featurette for the Cassie-and-Nate wedding episode of *Euphoria* Season 3 — Episode 3, titled "The Ballad of Paladin," which aired April 26, 2026 and immediately became one of the most discussed hours of television of the year. The featurette is built around the production of a single sequence: Cassie Howard (Sydney Sweeney) marrying Nate Jacobs (Jacob Elordi) in a Pinterest-saccharine ceremony that ends with Nate getting beaten up at his own front door, his pinky toe severed, and Cassie sobbing in a blood-flecked wedding gown. The voices stitched together across the thirteen minutes belong to the show's principal cast and creative leads. Sam Levinson, the show's creator and director, anchors the segment — discussing the violence-and-comedy register he was reaching for, the "actions have consequences" through-line of the season, and his deeply personal disclosure about the late Eric Dane (Cal Jacobs), who filmed his Season 3 scenes after revealing his ALS diagnosis to Levinson months before production began. Natasha Newman-Thomas, who replaced Heidi Bivens as costume designer for Season 3 after working with Levinson on *The Idol*, walks through the wedding looks: Cassie's 16-foot-train custom corset gown co-designed with New York designer Jackson Wiederhoeft and accessorized with eight-inch Jimmy Choo heels and half a million dollars in Cartier diamonds; Maddy Perez's (Alexa Demie) gravity-defying revenge dress, structured around a rosary that Newman-Thomas describes as "an Alexander Calder sculpture"; Lexi Howard's (Maude Apatow) vintage Nana Jacqueline pick; Jules Vaughn's (Hunter Schafer) Acne Studios spring 2023 runway sample; Nate's custom Bottega Veneta tuxedo. François Audouy, the season's production designer, explains how the wedding's color palette was generated by sampling pinks from a literal cooked shrimp, and how the Spanish Revival interior was built on a Warner Bros stage to match the Langham Huntington Pasadena exterior. Eric Dane describes Cal's three-act arc — meet Cal, see Cal, don't want to be Cal — and his decision to improvise Lloyd-the-bartender lines from Stanley Kubrick's *The Shining* into his final wedding scene with Hunter Schafer. Choreographer Ryan Heffington (the man behind Sia's "Chandelier") talks Sweeney and Elordi through their wedding dance. The featurette closes with the violence — VVDFX prosthetics for the toe-severing, Levinson's reference to the Coen Brothers and PT Anderson's mode of comic violence, and Cassie's famous "we just shoot her sitting there" reaction-take that became the episode's signature image.

key_quotes.quote: It's gravity-defying like an Alexander Calder sculpture. You're just like, how is the whole front of that gown balancing on this rosary?
key_quotes.context: Newman-Thomas is articulating her design solution for the most difficult costume challenge of the wedding episode — Maddy Perez's revenge dress, which had to compete with Cassie's 16-foot-train Wiederhoeft gown without dressing the bridesmaid as the bride. Her reach for the Calder-mobile metaphor is more precise than it sounds. Calder's hanging sculptures derive their visual drama from the impossibility of their balance — large planes of metal seeming to float without support, with the entire st

key_quotes.quote: It's a bold move to have the rosary hanging right above my tasteful bum. God can be anywhere.
key_quotes.context: Demie is describing the placement of the rosary in Maddy's revenge dress — hanging from the back of the neck and falling down the spine, with the cross landing at the small of her back, just above what she calls "my tasteful bum." The line is the featurette's funniest costume beat and a precise piece of self-aware Demie comedy. "Tasteful bum" is an oxymoron played for its full deadpan absurdity — a costume description that is also a piece of self-eulogy for a character whose entire screen person

key_quotes.quote: I told him that I was inspired by um a shrimp cocktail. So I literally sampled the pinks from a shrimp and then that became the color palette for the wedding...
key_quotes.context: Audouy, the production designer for Season 3, is explaining to camera how he generated the wedding's master color palette. The "him" is Sam Levinson, with whom Audouy had to negotiate a $50,000-flower budget that became the season's running plot point. Audouy's choice to use a literal cooked shrimp as a reference is doing two kinds of work at once. Practically, it gave him a self-contained palette — pinks, peaches, oranges, and corals at saturation — that read as warm and luxurious without cross

key_quotes.quote: I remember in the first two seasons thinking to myself, he cannot smile, like he does not smile. And this season his smile says so much about him.
key_quotes.context: Elordi is articulating a deliberate acting choice that has structured his three-season arc as Nate Jacobs. In Seasons 1 and 2, he made a hard rule for himself: Nate does not smile. The smile was outside the character's emotional vocabulary, a tell of vulnerability the character could not afford. For Season 3, with Nate engaged to and then married to Cassie, Elordi opens up the smile and uses it as a specific emotional event. The choice is the kind of long-game character logic that working televi

key_quotes.quote: I really love Eric. A lot... maybe four or five months before we started shooting, he reached out to me and you know, told me about his diagnosis... I just said, we're going to make it all work.
key_quotes.context: Levinson is publicly accounting for the collaborative decision that defined Eric Dane's final on-screen performance — to film Cal Jacobs's Season 3 storyline with Dane's real-world ALS symptoms folded directly into the character. The chronology matters. Dane disclosed his ALS to Levinson four-or-five months before the April 2025 production start, and publicly announced his diagnosis in 2025. He died in February 2026, less than two months before this BTS aired. Levinson's framing here — "whatever

key_quotes.quote: The evolution of Cal is linear. There's act one where you get to meet Cal. There's act two where you get to see Cal. There's act three where you don't want to be Cal.
key_quotes.context: Dane is articulating the three-season arc of Cal Jacobs in the cleanest possible structural terms — and locating his Season 3 work specifically in the third act, the act where the audience has accumulated enough information to recoil from the character. Cal in Season 1 was the upstanding community figure whose hidden dimensions began surfacing late in the season; Cal in Season 2 was the man whose hidden life was forced into the open and whose family imploded around the disclosure; Cal in Season

key_quotes.quote: Say this repeat after me. Okay. Your money's no good here, Mr. Torrance.
key_quotes.context: This is Dane improvising on set as Cal Jacobs, channeling Lloyd, the phantom bartender from Stanley Kubrick's *The Shining*, into his wedding scene with Hunter Schafer's Jules. Lloyd in The Shining is one of cinema's most haunting images of charm-as-residue — a bartender who appears in the empty Overlook Hotel to serve Jack Torrance free drinks, possibly hallucinated, possibly possessed, definitely a man whose smile has outlasted his soul. Dane's choice to teach Jules the line — making her repea

key_quotes.quote: I felt like I was in that sequence in Deer Hunter.
key_quotes.context: One of the principal cast members — speaking about the wedding's communal line dance, in which Sydney Sweeney, Jacob Elordi, Alexa Demie, and the rest of the ensemble shot dance footage for twenty straight minutes — reaches for the most precise cinematic comparison available: the wedding sequence in Michael Cimino's *The Deer Hunter*. The reference is exact and slightly devastating. The wedding scene in *The Deer Hunter* is roughly an hour of screen time, the longest sustained set piece in Ameri

key_quotes.quote: What this season's about is actions have consequences and you can only outrun your mistakes for so long.
key_quotes.context: Levinson is articulating the central claim of *Euphoria* Season 3 in the cleanest possible terms — that the season is structured around the moment when fantasy gives way to consequence. The framing is doing specific work for the wedding episode in particular. Cassie has spent three seasons treating reality as something she can decorate around; the wedding is her largest decoration project, financed in part by an OnlyFans career, and it ends with her sitting on the floor in a blood-flecked gown.

PLQe_d5uj7c.title: Federico Fellini - Chaos In The Land of Memory
PLQe_d5uj7c.synopsis: The House of Tabula's 34-minute essay on Federico Fellini argues that the Italian master built his entire body of work on a contradiction: a director who began in the documentary realism of the post-war Italian Neorealism movement, who then spent the rest of his career insisting that the only honest way to capture reality was through pure artifice. The essay traces Fellini's path from his early work as a cartoonist and screenwriter alongside Roberto Rossellini on *Rome Open City* and *Paisan* through his rise as the international face of the cinematic auteur — the figure so identified with his medium that he was simply called *Il Maestro*. The video walks through the recurring grammar of his cinema — the circus, the cavalcade of humanity, the clown beneath the mask, the saturated frame in which sacred and profane coexist without judgment — and locates its origin in his cartoonist's habit of reaching truth through caricature rather than archetype. The essay then turns to Fellini's working method: his second home at Studio 5 inside Cinecittà, his deliberate inversion of location and set, his refusal to give actors full scripts, his Bressonian habit of speaking to performers from off-camera while the Italian post-dubbing tradition let him replace their words later. The final movement traces Fellini's progressive devouring of his own filmography — from observer to alter ego (Marcello Mastroianni's Guido in *8½*) to literal subject of his own movies (*Intervista*) — and closes on the argument that Fellini's chaos is not chaos at all but the only form of order that lets memory, imagination, and reality occupy the same frame at once. The argument running underneath is that the word "Paparazzi" entered the global lexicon from a single Fellini character because Fellini's imagined Rome ultimately rewrote the real one.

key_quotes.quote: In a medium that's all about seeing things, is the role of the director to remain unseen? Yet there are a select few that for whatever reason transcend this quasi-incognito duty, and the mere suggestion of their presence towers over the medium ad infinitum.
key_quotes.context: The opening line of the entire essay, framing the rest as an argument about which directors transcend the standard invisibility of the role and why Fellini was the most extreme case of that transcendence.

key_quotes.quote: There's an innate duality within Fellini's work that many times contradicts itself — the love of the church and the love of decadence. But rather than finding the reasons behind it, Fellini simply lets them live with equivalence in his frames.
key_quotes.context: The cleanest formulation of Fellini's central ethical move: refusing to pass judgment on contradictory aspects of Italian life and instead letting them coexist on the same plane.

key_quotes.quote: All of this would be overwhelming for most, but Fellini embraces this chaos, for through his lens, this living is a thing of beauty.
key_quotes.context: Discussing the twelve-minute outdoor banquet sequence in *Roma*, where the camera finds aesthetic value in the noisy, argumentative, vibrant disorder of communal Italian life rather than imposing structure on it.

key_quotes.quote: Fellini believes in a love of life. The passion exudes from every aspect of his films, and it's why everyone is capable of redemption.
key_quotes.context: The video's summary of Fellini's humanism, framed against the cruel Zampanò in *La Strada* — even the most callous characters are eventually offered a moment of confronting their own duplicity and a path back toward humanity.

key_quotes.quote: Fellini was constantly threatened by his own artificiality. He garnered a name for himself almost as a persona with a nickname, a costume, and a reputation. Much like the caricatures he drew growing up, he became one himself.
key_quotes.context: The essay's pivot to Fellini's self-construction. The essay argues that the public Fellini persona — the fedora, the white scarf, *Il Maestro* — was as much a hyper-stylized creation as anything in his films, and that the director understood this about himself.

key_quotes.quote: Fellini is creating pure fantasy worlds because to him, this is the only way we can experience a true reality — a reality that is the subjective experience of one attempting to capture the universal in us all.
key_quotes.context: The clearest articulation of the essay's central philosophical claim about Fellini's relationship to artifice. Literal documentation fails because reproduction is unavoidable; only synthetic reproduction tuned to subjective memory can reach a deeper kind of truth.

key_quotes.quote: This balance between artificiality and reality is at the crook of all of Fellini's work — the notion of a complete contradiction of opposing forces attempting to coexist with one another. Naive women and insecure men, the rule of the Catholic Church and the drive for absolute freedom. Fellini's imagination is often at war with itself.
key_quotes.context: Describing Studio 5 at Cinecittà and the deliberate inversion of where Fellini chose to shoot interior versus exterior scenes. The contradictions visible in the frames are presented as the same contradictions that organized his entire production method.

key_quotes.quote: The only thing that seems to be the uniting force with his methodology is that Fellini was the one in control. Fellini wants to be in charge across every stage of the creative process.
key_quotes.context: The essay's deconstruction of the Fellini-as-improviser myth. What looked like instinctual chaos on set was actually the surface of an extremely controlled artistic process in which actors were treated as tools rather than collaborators.

key_quotes.quote: It is just that I think that my way to live, my way to realize myself, more congenial, more near to my nature, more — and even less difficult — is just to to to create images. So that is the reason why I make pictures. There is no no no other reason.
key_quotes.context: A rare archival clip of Fellini speaking in his own voice in English about why he directed films. The hesitations and repetitions are part of what makes the quote land — the most influential auteur in mid-century cinema reduces his entire vocation to a simple, almost sheepish admission that making images was the only thing that came naturally to him.

key_quotes.quote: He grew so large that he devoured his work to the point his movies were an appendage of him. Whatever deconstructions, dream sequences, or carnivals made up his films, one thing always remained — it was undeniably Fellini.
key_quotes.context: The essay's final formulation of the late-career arc. By the time Fellini reaches *Intervista*, he has not just become the subject of his films — he has subsumed them entirely, so that the films exist as extensions of the man rather than separate works.

d1On1CUXdrg.title: Federico Fellini’s 8½: His Greatest Film & A Cinematic Revolution🎞️
d1On1CUXdrg.synopsis: A four-minute and fifty-five second tribute reel from the LK International Film Festival on Federico Fellini's 1963 masterpiece 8½. The format is the canonical festival-programming-reel structure: brief biographical setup, montage of clips with Nino Rota's score underneath, voiceover summary of the film's themes, archival footage of the 1964 Academy Awards ceremony at which 8½ won Best Foreign Language Film, Fellini's actual acceptance speech delivered in his accented English ("I am particularly happy to receive this fabulous award because it comes to me on the eve of my next picture"), and a closing benediction over the film as "a dream, a memory, a reflection of all of us." The reel's surface is brief. Its territory is enormous. Fellini directed twenty-four features across a forty-five year career, won four Best Foreign Language Film Oscars (a record), the Palme d'Or, the Career Golden Lion at Venice, and an Honorary Oscar for Lifetime Achievement in 1993. He has an adjective named after him — Felliniesque. 8½ is the film that gave the design vocabulary for every subsequent film about a film director with creative block — Bob Fosse's All That Jazz, Woody Allen's Stardust Memories, François Truffaut's Day for Night, Charlie Kaufman's Synecdoche New York, Alejandro González Iñárritu's Birdman, and Paolo Sorrentino's The Great Beauty. The musical Nine adapted it to Broadway in 1982 and Rob Marshall adapted that musical back to film in 2009. The reel is short. The map is global.

key_quotes.quote: There is no end. There is no beginning. There is only the infinite passion of life.
key_quotes.context: The opening epigraph of the LK International Film Festival reel attributes one of Fellini's most-circulated aphorisms to its source. The line is a short statement of Fellini's working philosophy — that narrative cinema's bias toward beginnings and endings is itself a kind of dishonesty, and that the actual texture of life is recursive, looping, and resistant to closure. 8½ is the film in which Fellini built that philosophy directly into the film's structure: Guido Anselmi never finishes his proj

key_quotes.quote: Imagine a world where dreams, memories, and reality collide. This is the universe of 8½.
key_quotes.context: The reel's narrator opens its description of the film with the textbook definition of the Felliniesque mode. The line is the cleanest one-sentence summary in circulation of what 8½ does formally — collapses three temporal registers (past memory, present narrative, fantasized future) into a single continuous visual surface. The historical context is that this collapse was not a standard cinematic device when 8½ was released in 1963. Hollywood films of the period marked dream sequences with explic

key_quotes.quote: 8½ is a film about more than just filmmaking. It's a reflection on the human condition, on self-doubt, love, and the search for meaning.
key_quotes.context: The voiceover argues that 8½'s subject is not its surface plot — a film director with creative block trying to make a film — but the larger condition of being an adult human at midlife, surrounded by competing demands and unable to satisfy any of them. The line is the reel's case for why audiences who are not film directors should still watch the film. Fellini was forty-three when he made 8½ and the film is unmistakably a midlife-crisis document. Guido Anselmi has a wife he is failing, a mistres

key_quotes.quote: It's an emotional roller coaster, with each frame a work of art in itself.
key_quotes.context: The voiceover credits 8½ with frame-by-frame compositional rigor — a quality usually reserved for still photography or for filmmakers like Stanley Kubrick whose visual perfectionism is part of their critical reputation. The framing is accurate and points at cinematographer Gianni Di Venanzo, whose deep-focus, high-contrast black-and-white photography on 8½ is the actual physical reason the reel can plausibly claim that each frame is "a work of art in itself." Di Venanzo had previously shot Miche

key_quotes.quote: And the winner is Italy for Federico Fellini, 8½.
key_quotes.context: The archival footage from the 36th Academy Awards on April 13, 1964, captures the moment 8½ was named winner of Best Foreign Language Film. Fellini had been on the Academy's radar for nearly a decade by this point: La Strada won the same category in 1956, Nights of Cabiria won in 1957, and La Dolce Vita won in 1960. The 1963 win for 8½ made Fellini the four-time category record holder, a record he extended to five wins in 1974 with Amarcord and that has never been broken. The other Italian compe

key_quotes.quote: I am particularly happy to receive this fabulous award because it comes to me on the eve of my next picture, and I don't think that a director about to start his new job could wish for anything more stimulating and auspicious of this.
key_quotes.context: Fellini accepts the Best Foreign Language Film Oscar for 8½ at the 36th Academy Awards in his accented English — formal, halting, sincere. The "next picture" he references is Juliet of the Spirits (Giulietta degli Spiriti, 1965), the first color film of his career, starring his wife Giulietta Masina as a 40-something woman who summons the visions and mysticism necessary to leave her cheating husband. Juliet is the female-perspective companion to 8½'s male-perspective inquiry — same fragmented dr

key_quotes.quote: I want you all to accept my deepest and more humble thanks.
key_quotes.context: Fellini closes his Best Foreign Language Film acceptance with the Italian construction "more humble" rather than the English idiom "most humble." The slip is the kind of native-Italian-speaker-translating-on-the-fly mistake that small audio recordings of Fellini's speeches are full of. The line lands as endearing rather than awkward because Fellini was, by 1964, already understood by American audiences to be primarily a working artist and only secondarily a public personality. His English was go

key_quotes.quote: This isn't just a film. It's a dream. A memory. A reflection of all of us.
key_quotes.context: The closing benediction of the reel is the festival programmer's case for why 8½ continues to matter sixty-three years after its release. The framing — "a reflection of all of us" — is supposed to land as universalism. It mostly does. The qualification, which the reel doesn't make, is that Fellini's "all of us" was relatively narrow: a heterosexual middle-aged Italian man with Catholic guilt, a working wife, a mistress, an absent producer, and a fading memory of his mother and his prostitute. 5Gm3-wcMeFc.title: Four Favorites with Barbie Ferreira, Devon Bostick, Isaiah Lehtinen and Chandler Levack
5Gm3-wcMeFc.synopsis: The Letterboxd Four Favorites format normally tapes one subject. This installment tapes four — Chandler Levack (writer-director), Barbie Ferreira (lead), Devon Bostick, and Isaiah Lehtinen. The quartet is a Mile End Kicks press junket: Levack's second feature, premiered at TIFF 2025, theatrical April 17, 2026, in which Ferreira plays Grace Pine, an aspiring music writer who moves to Montreal's Mile End neighborhood. The picks are full of receipts. Levack names Cameron Crowe's Almost Famous as the film that "made me want to drop out of university and become a rock critic, which I did when I was 19" — and Mile End Kicks is the dramatization of those years, the love letter she calls it on tape. Lehtinen, who broke out as Lawrence Kweller in Levack's I Like Movies (2022), names Spike Jonze's Adaptation, the screenwriting-about-screenwriting reference in a film that's loosely autobiographical for the writer-director who cast him. Ferreira, post-Euphoria and producing her own work, names Sofia Coppola's Lost in Translation — another small-format film by a young woman finding herself somewhere she didn't grow up. Bostick names Satoshi Kon's Perfect Blue and calls it "basically a movie about my life." The four-favorites bit is the public marketing ledger of a film about young people becoming themselves through criticism, music, and obsessive cinephilia.

key_quotes.quote: I watched that movie when I was 15, and I was like, how do I live inside this film forever? It's what made me want to drop out of university and become a rock critic, which I did when I was 19.
key_quotes.context: Levack is naming the precise mechanism by which Cameron Crowe's 2000 Almost Famous changed her life. The film is the autobiographical document of Crowe's own teenage years writing for Rolling Stone in 1973, dramatized via Patrick Fugit's William Miller and Kate Hudson's Penny Lane and Lester Bangs as played by Philip Seymour Hoffman. Levack saw it as a fifteen-year-old in Brantford, Ontario, read the film as a how-to document, and at nineteen acted on it — dropping out of cinema studies at the U

key_quotes.quote: It's like this movie is kind of a love letter and a tribute to that film.
key_quotes.context: Levack is making the connection between Almost Famous and Mile End Kicks explicit on camera, in the same breath as the dropout-and-became-a-rock-critic story. The pairing is precise. Both films are autobiographical accounts by writer-directors of their own years as young rock critics — Crowe wrote his at age forty-three about his own fifteen-year-old self; Levack wrote hers at forty about her own twenty-two-year-old self. Both films are about young people who use music criticism as the disguise

key_quotes.quote: I left that going like, oh, I've just had a profound experience, and I didn't know that you could just make movies like that.
key_quotes.context: Lehtinen is talking about Bob Fosse's 1979 All That Jazz — Fosse's autobiographical film about a Broadway choreographer-director (Roy Scheider as Joe Gideon) chain-smoking and womanizing his way through an open-heart surgery while editing a film and choreographing a musical, structured around hallucinated conversations with the angel of death played by Jessica Lange. The film won the Palme d'Or at Cannes in 1980 and remains one of the most formally radical American studio musicals ever made. Leh

key_quotes.quote: The neuroses of screenwriting and it being in like being an artist versus hitting the hero's journey.
key_quotes.context: Lehtinen is articulating the central conflict of Spike Jonze's 2002 Adaptation — Charlie Kaufman's screenplay about Charlie Kaufman's failure to adapt Susan Orlean's The Orchid Thief, with Nicolas Cage playing Charlie and his invented twin Donald, and Robert McKee's Story screenwriting seminars literally appearing in the film as a third-act intervention. The "hero's journey" reference is direct: the film stages a debate with itself about whether to follow the Joseph Campbell template McKee teach

key_quotes.quote: These are the two most iconic badass people I've ever seen in my life. Susan Sarandon icon, and Geena Davis obviously.
key_quotes.context: Lehtinen is delivering the only big-format Hollywood pick of his four — Ridley Scott's 1991 Thelma & Louise, the Callie Khouri-written road movie about a waitress (Sarandon's Louise) and a housewife (Davis's Thelma) who shoot a rapist outside an Arkansas roadhouse and drive toward Mexico. The "icon" framing is the working actor's vocabulary — both Sarandon and Davis built post-Thelma careers as activist actresses with sharp public personas, Sarandon for left politics and Davis for the Geena Davi

key_quotes.quote: It's like basically a movie about my life, pretty much.
key_quotes.context: Bostick is naming Satoshi Kon's 1997 animated thriller Perfect Blue — the film about a J-pop idol named Mima Kirigoe who quits her group CHAM! to become a serious actress and is stalked by an obsessive fan, her own former pop self, and a fragmenting sense of which Mima is real. The film was a major influence on Darren Aronofsky's Black Swan and Requiem for a Dream. Bostick was Rodrick Heffley in three Diary of a Wimpy Kid films starting at eighteen and Jasper Jordan on The CW's The 100 from twen

key_quotes.quote: I'm so happy it's getting like English distribution and stuff.
key_quotes.context: Bostick is closing his pick of Hideaki Anno's 1998 Love & Pop — Anno's first live-action film, made immediately after directing Neon Genesis Evangelion (1995-1996) and The End of Evangelion (1997). Love & Pop adapts Ryu Murakami's novel about four Tokyo high school girls engaged in enjo kosai (compensated dating) and shoots almost entirely on a first-person consumer-grade Sony digital camcorder, two years before Lars von Trier's Dogme 95 collective made the same digital austerity an internationa

maI4BMPXzUg.title: Four Favorites with Bob Odenkirk (Normal)
maI4BMPXzUg.synopsis: Bob Odenkirk sits for Letterboxd's Four Favorites series during the press cycle for *Normal* — the Ben Wheatley action thriller written by Derek Kolstad that opened in 2,000 theaters on April 17, 2026, the widest release in Magnolia Pictures' twenty-four-year history. He opens with a defense of the theatrical experience itself, suggesting that audiences are rediscovering what theaters offer, and anchors that argument in his childhood. He picks George Lucas's *American Graffiti* as the first feature film he ever saw twice in a theater, watching it at a long-gone movie house in Naperville, Illinois, and reports that he met Lucas just a few days earlier at the Saturn Awards. He picks Ingmar Bergman's *Fanny and Alexander* as a downtown-Chicago childhood memory — riding the L train into the city as a nine or ten-year-old with his Aunt Mary, possibly to the Chicago Theater. He picks Wim Wenders's *Wings of Desire* as the film he saw with his friend Jeff Garlin and hated at the time for being heavy-handed, only to find that he could never forget it and has been thinking about its themes for forty years. He closes with Lasse Hallström's *My Life as a Dog*, which he first caught while doing a week of stand-up in Kalamazoo, Michigan, and has revisited recently — and he reports that when he raved about the film to a friend in public, the person at the next table turned out to be Paul Thomas Anderson, who agreed it was a great film. The clip is short but it traces forty years of one actor's relationship with the theater, from a Naperville matinee in 1973 through a Chicago L-train trip to Bergman, through a heavy-handed German art-house movie he could not shake, to a Swedish coming-of-age film that turned into an unexpected co-sign from one of the most respected American filmmakers working today.

key_quotes.quote: We're missing something by not going to the movies. Movies can still be very special. In a weird way, maybe they're becoming special again. So maybe people are rediscovering theaters.
key_quotes.context: Odenkirk's opening defense of the theatrical experience and the load-bearing pitch of the entire interview. The line lands harder when you remember he is sitting for this Letterboxd interview to promote *Normal*, the Ben Wheatley action film that opened in 2,000 theaters the week of April 17, 2026 — the widest release in Magnolia Pictures' history.

key_quotes.quote: American Graffiti. It's the first feature film that I saw twice in the theater.
key_quotes.context: The cleanest distillation of Odenkirk's argument that theaters matter. He saw *American Graffiti* in 1973 at a Naperville movie house that no longer exists, loved it enough to go back a week later, and is still telling the story fifty-three years later — the same week he reports meeting George Lucas at the Saturn Awards.

key_quotes.quote: She did not understand the concept of going twice to the same movie. That made no sense to her.
key_quotes.context: The funniest moment in the interview and the line that turns Odenkirk's mother into the audience surrogate for everyone who treats movies as one-and-done entertainment. The eleven-year-old Odenkirk had already discovered something about cinema that his mother's generation had not, and the story still gets a laugh half a century later.

key_quotes.quote: I remember seeing Wings of Desire with my friend Jeff Garlin and hating the movie but never forgetting it and thinking about it often and about the themes of it. It's a great film.
key_quotes.context: The most genuinely contrarian critical position in the interview. Wenders's 1987 art-house masterpiece is one of the most canonized European films of the late twentieth century, and Odenkirk is admitting he disliked it on first viewing while declaring it great anyway. The Jeff Garlin detail confirms the screening took place in the late 1980s, when Odenkirk and Garlin were both Chicago-based comedians.

key_quotes.quote: I remember thinking, 'This is obvious. The theme is so heavy-handed.' But it's kind of a big theme and it is worthy of contemplation and the patience that it's approached with.
key_quotes.context: Odenkirk's full critical reversal on *Wings of Desire* in two sentences. The first sentence is the eighteen-year-old reaction, the second is the sixty-three-year-old correction. The implicit principle — that "heavy-handed" is sometimes the right approach to a big theme — is the most useful piece of viewing advice in the whole interview.

key_quotes.quote: My Life as a Dog, sweet film, and I saw it when I was doing stand-up in Kalamazoo, Michigan. I was doing a week of stand-up and I went to see that in the theater. It was such a good movie. It really impacted me.
key_quotes.context: The most personally specific recollection of the interview. The Kalamazoo stand-up tour places the screening in the late 1980s, before *Saturday Night Live* and *Mr. Show*, when Odenkirk was still working the road as a stand-up. The detail of going to a movie alone on a stand-up tour is the working comedian's version of the trip downtown to see Bergman.

key_quotes.quote: And it was Paul Thomas Anderson who was listening, overhearing me rave about My Life as a Dog and saying, 'Oh, yeah, that's a great film.'
key_quotes.context: The closing anecdote and the kind of story that sounds invented but is not. The director of *Boogie Nights*, *Magnolia*, *There Will Be Blood*, *Phantom Thread*, and the 2025 release *One Battle After Another* turns out to be at the next table when Odenkirk is raving about a small Swedish coming-of-age movie from 1985. The line is also the cleanest possible payoff for the entire Letterboxd Four Favorites premise — a working filmmaker confirming a working actor's choice.

SS5IzLd3fHo.title: Four Favorites with Michaela Coel (The Christophers, Mother Mary)
SS5IzLd3fHo.synopsis: Michaela Coel sits for Letterboxd's Four Favorites series during the rare press cycle for two simultaneous 2026 releases — Steven Soderbergh's *The Christophers*, in which she plays art forger Lori Butler opposite Ian McKellen, and David Lowery's A24 psychodrama *Mother Mary*, in which she plays costume designer Sam Anselm opposite Anne Hathaway. Both films opened in U.S. theaters in April 2026. Coel chooses four pictures that have shaped her work as a writer, actor, and director. She praises the writing of Bryan Bertino's 2016 horror film *The Monster*. She picks Andrey Zvyagintsev's *Leviathan* and *Loveless* together, and singles out a line from *Loveless* — "you are God and the Devil rolled into one" — as the philosophical foundation for how she wrote her HBO/BBC limited series *I May Destroy You*, the show that earned her the 2021 Emmy for Outstanding Writing for a Limited Series and made her the first Black woman to win in that category. She closes with Ari Aster's *Hereditary*, which she watched alone at 2 a.m. with a little weed in her system and which gave her a clinical panic attack the next day. The clip is short but it traces the artistic lineage of one of the most consequential British writer-creators working today, from Russian art-house bleakness through the new wave of A24 horror, with a working philosophy — that humans are simultaneously god and devil, that the films you cannot forget are the ones that wrecked you — that connects her writing for *Chewing Gum* and *I May Destroy You* to the new work in *The Christophers*, *Mother Mary*, and her upcoming HBO/BBC series *First Day on Earth*.

key_quotes.quote: The Monster, beautiful writing.
key_quotes.context: Coel's complete endorsement of Bryan Bertino's 2016 horror film, in two words. The brevity is the point — she is not praising scares or atmosphere, she is praising the screenplay, which is the read of a working writer who notices the script underneath the genre.

key_quotes.quote: There's a line I remember from Loveless, which is 'you are God and the Devil rolled into one.'
key_quotes.context: The single most consequential critical claim in the interview. Coel pulls one line of dialogue from Andrey Zvyagintsev's 2017 film and credits it as the philosophical foundation for *I May Destroy You* — the limited series that earned her the 2021 Emmy for Outstanding Writing for a Limited Series and made her the first Black woman to win in that category.

key_quotes.quote: Of healing, of anger, of being a hurt person who has hurt other people, and it kind of changed my lens and how I look at humans.
key_quotes.context: The most personally autobiographical sentence in the interview. The "hurt person who has hurt other people" framing is the working philosophy of her entire writing career — *Chewing Gum*, *I May Destroy You*, the 2018 MacTaggart Lecture, *Misfits: A Personal Manifesto*, and the upcoming *First Day on Earth* all run on this principle.

key_quotes.quote: The score is barely there. So you can't tell whether this is the score of the film or whether there's something in your house.
key_quotes.context: Coel's precise formal criticism of *Hereditary*'s sound design. Colin Stetson's saxophone-and-electronics score for the 2018 film works at such low volumes that the audience genuinely cannot distinguish it from ambient sound — which is the design principle that made the film work on Coel and on every other viewer who watched it alone.

key_quotes.quote: Then I started texting my friend like, 'Oh, this movie is really scary. Are you awake?' He wasn't awake.
key_quotes.context: The funniest beat in the entire interview and the perfect illustration of what good horror does to people who watch it alone at 2 a.m. The fact that the friend was not awake is the punchline; the fact that Coel needed someone to be awake is the actual subject of the anecdote.

key_quotes.quote: The next day I had a panic attack and that's why it's one of my favorite movies.
key_quotes.context: The clearest possible inversion of standard horror-criticism rules. Coel measures *Hereditary* not by craft or originality but by clinical bodily impact. The fact that the film produced an actual physiological symptom twenty-four hours later is the reason she considers it one of her favorites.

key_quotes.quote: If you can do that to me, if you could fuck me up like that, now you're winning. So yeah, Ari Aster for the win.
key_quotes.context: The closing line of the entire video and the cleanest possible verdict on Aster's debut. The philosophy is direct: a horror movie that fundamentally rearranges the viewer's nervous system has earned its place. Aster has gone on to make *Midsommar*, *Beau Is Afraid*, and *Eddington*, but *Hereditary* remains the film that proved his method, and Coel's anecdote is the cleanest piece of evidence for why.

JjjKKXkM38w.title: Four Favorites with Patrick Ball, Shabana Azeez, Supriya Ganesh, Katherine LaNasa and Taylor Dearden
JjjKKXkM38w.synopsis: The Letterboxd Four Favorites format normally tapes one subject. This installment tapes five — Patrick Ball, Shabana Azeez, Supriya Ganesh, Katherine LaNasa, and Taylor Dearden — the principal cast of HBO Max's The Pitt, which just wrapped its second season on April 16, 2026. Season 1 swept the 2025 Emmys (Outstanding Drama Series, Outstanding Casting, plus Lead Actor for Noah Wyle, Supporting Actress for Katherine LaNasa, and Guest Actor for Shawn Hatosy). The taping catches all five at the moment they've collectively become Hollywood's freshest ensemble. Patrick Ball delivers the freshest take of the visit — Andy's boyfriend Nate is the real villain of The Devil Wears Prada — and includes Sentimental Value in his four because of "currency." Taylor Dearden has watched Sentimental Value four times because of how miscasting drives the plot, and confirmed her reading directly with Elle Fanning. Shabana Azeez announces Shrek as her fourth pick because, as a "funny brown girl" growing up in Adelaide, the green ogre was her representation. Supriya Ganesh, a Columbia neuroscience grad who scored in the 99th percentile on the MCAT before becoming an actress, picks Bong Joon-ho's Parasite as her obvious first. Katherine LaNasa, an Emmy winner at fifty-nine after a thirty-year career, is asked to pick her character's favorites instead of her own. The bit is a working group of actors caught at the hinge moment between regional theater, Australian indie features, neuroscience tutoring, and prestige television fame. The picks are the receipts.

9huJIGPUKQ4.title: In the Screening Room with Martin Scorsese and Leonardo DiCaprio
9huJIGPUKQ4.synopsis: A Letterboxd "In the Screening Room" conversation in which Martin Scorsese and Leonardo DiCaprio walk through the films Scorsese screened for his cast and crew during their six collaborations together. The format is intimate — two chairs, no audience, Marty in his usual all-black, Leo in a casual sweater, the two of them swapping references the way two friends do when they have known each other for almost twenty-five years. The throughline is Scorsese's working practice of using cinema history as creative pedagogy: rather than describe the tone he wants for a scene, he assembles the cast and crew in a screening room and shows them the film he is trying to channel. His Girl Friday for the dining-room sequence in The Aviator. Hell's Angels for The Aviator's aerial work. Andrzej Wajda's Ashes and Diamonds and Zbigniew Cybulski's performance for The Departed. Laura, Crossfire, and Out of the Past for the noir paranoia underneath Shutter Island. Stanley Kubrick's Barry Lyndon for the period authenticity of Gangs of New York. Howard Hawks's pre-code Scarface for The Wolf of Wall Street. George Stevens's A Place in the Sun and William Wyler's The Heiress for the twisted Ernest-Mollie marriage at the heart of Killers of the Flower Moon. The conversation is recorded after Killers of the Flower Moon's release, with both men still actively working: DiCaprio is now coming off the global success of Paul Thomas Anderson's One Battle After Another, the 2025 Pynchon adaptation that swept the 98th Academy Awards in March 2026 with six wins including Best Picture; Scorsese is mid-second-season on Martin Scorsese Presents: The Saints, the Fox Nation docudrama series he developed with Kent Jones, and is in development on a feature about the life of Jesus.

key_quotes.quote: I was asked what films I introduced to you, but considering you've seen every film ever made up until 1980, it's pretty hard to say — other than maybe Spirited Away, Miyazaki's films, and maybe Princess Mononoke. It was Spirited Away that you told me to watch.
key_quotes.context: DiCaprio opens the conversation with the only example in the entire interview where the recommendation runs from younger to older instead of the other way around. The "every film ever made up until 1980" framing lands as an affectionate exaggeration but contains a real working observation about Scorsese's cinema history. key_quotes.quote: The speed of the picture is only equaled by that movie Baby Face — maybe Goodfellas. Maybe Goodfellas.
key_quotes.context: Scorsese ranks his own Goodfellas alongside Howard Hawks's His Girl Friday and the pre-code Baby Face for sheer pace of dialogue and cutting. The hesitation in his repetition — "maybe Goodfellas, maybe Goodfellas" — is the working filmmaker self-correcting in real time, deciding whether to claim a place in the lineage or let Hawks have the title. The framing matters more than the comparison. Goodfellas's compressed voice-over, the fast-cut Copacabana long-take with overlapping dialogue, and the

key_quotes.quote: It's all like an extraordinary string quartet of some kind. You could see this in any Welles film, but primarily you really get it in Howard Hawks.
key_quotes.context: Scorsese's chamber-music metaphor for the Hawks-Welles overlap-dialogue technique is the most precise framing in the conversation. Chamber music's defining feature is that no single instrument carries the meaning — the texture emerges from the ensemble. The same is true of Hawks-Welles overlap: no single line in His Girl Friday or the early reels of Citizen Kane carries the plot weight, and the meaning is constructed by the audience reassembling fragments from the wash of overlapping speech. key_quotes.quote: The reason why it appeared so slow is that there was nothing going by. It was all flat prairie. Hence the airplanes against the clouds.
key_quotes.context: The Killers of the Flower Moon location-scouting story is one of the best anecdotes in Scorsese's vast working life as a public filmmaker. He recalls being driven across Oklahoma at what he thought was forty miles per hour — until he looked at the speedometer and saw seventy-five. The realization is a direct callback to Howard Hughes's 1930 Hell's Angels: Hughes needed clouds in the sky to give his aerial dogfights any sense of motion or scale, because flying a plane against a clear sky gives th

key_quotes.quote: He would have been as known as Mastroianni or any of the great European or Eastern European actors who became international, but he was tragically killed shooting a film a couple of years later, running onto a train, a running train. He fell. It was a great loss. But he was considered the European James Dean.
key_quotes.context: Scorsese's footnote on Zbigniew Cybulski is one of the most poignant moments in the interview. Cybulski died on January 8, 1967, at thirty-nine, while attempting to jump onto a moving train at Wrocław Railway Station — exactly the kind of physical action that defined his onscreen image as the European James Dean. The hat detail is exact: Andrews wears the hat indoors throughout the film, a 1940s convention that has aged into a noir-iconography signature. The "corruption of the American dream" framing is precise: Stevens's George Eastman murders his pregnant working-class girlfriend so he can marry into wealth, and the film's tragedy is that Eastman never quite admits to himself that this is what he is doing. The directing decision is the one William Wyler made at the end of The Heiress: the betrayed party stands up and walks out, and the betrayer is left to finish reading the consequences of his own behavior on his own face. The two of them have spent sixteen and a half minutes walking through a working pedagogy, and the only summary the conversation needs is the one DiCaprio offers: the time is up, the partnership continues, the next picture is whatever it turns out to be. tVbC_8kLdso.title: Inside George Nakashima’s Japanese-Style Home & Workshop | Unique Spaces | Architectural Digest
tVbC_8kLdso.synopsis: Mira Nakashima — daughter of George Nakashima, born in Seattle in 1942, transferred at six weeks old to the Minidoka concentration camp in the Idaho desert, and now president and creative director of George Nakashima Woodworkers — gives Architectural Digest a personal tour of the New Hope, Pennsylvania property her father built with his own two hands. The house, the Conoid Studio, the Arts Building, the Reception House, the chair shop, the lumber barn — all of it sits on three acres George traded for labor with a fellow MIT graduate in the early 1940s, while Mira's father lived in an army tent. The story Mira tells is one of the great American architectural lives. George Nakashima was born in 1905 in Spokane, studied forestry then architecture at the University of Washington, took a master's in architecture from MIT in 1930, sold his car in the depths of the Great Depression for a round-the-world steamship ticket, watched Le Corbusier's Pavillon Suisse rise in Paris, joined Antonin Raymond's office in Tokyo in 1934, supervised the construction of the Sri Aurobindo Ashram's Golconde dormitory in Pondicherry from 1936 to 1939 (the first reinforced-concrete building in India), became a disciple of Sri Aurobindo and was given the Sanskrit name Sundarananda — "one who delights in beauty" — returned to America in 1941, married Marion Okajima, was incarcerated at Minidoka in 1942 with his infant daughter Mira, learned Japanese joinery from master carpenter Gentaro Hikogawa inside the camp using packing-crate scrap and bitter brush, was sponsored out of the camp in 1943 by Antonin Raymond who had himself fled Tokyo for a chicken farm in Bucks County, and finally — having concluded "chickens and I were not psychologically compatible" — built the New Hope studio that would become the cradle of the American Craft Movement. He died in 1990 having received the Third Order of the Sacred Treasure from the Emperor of Japan and become friends with Isamu Noguchi, Harry Bertoia, Ben Shahn, and Junzo Yoshimura. Mira walks us through the buildings he made and the materials he loved.

key_quotes.quote: Every inch of that cathedral, it was built by people of all different walks of life who just worked together to build this cathedral.
key_quotes.context: Mira is describing what her father loved about Chartres Cathedral when he saw it during his Depression-era stay in Paris in 1932. The cathedral, built from 1194 to 1220 across multiple generations by stonemasons, glaziers, woodcarvers, and laborers whose names were not recorded, became a permanent reference point for George Nakashima's anti-ego philosophy of making. The Cathedral of Notre-Dame de Chartres is the textbook example of medieval collective authorship — the architectural opposite of t

key_quotes.quote: He became very involved in the society and the philosophy and took on the name of Sundarananda.
key_quotes.context: Mira is telling the central spiritual fact of her father's life — that during his years in Pondicherry from 1936 to 1939, working as the on-site architect for the Golconde dormitory, George Nakashima became a disciple of Sri Aurobindo, the Bengali nationalist, philosopher, and yogi who founded the ashram in 1926. Sri Aurobindo's spiritual partner was Mirra Alfassa, known to ashramites as the Mother. The Sanskrit name Sundarananda — "one who delights in beauty" — was given to Nakashima by the ash

key_quotes.quote: Dad, he knew how to make the best of whatever was given you.
key_quotes.context: Mira is summarizing her father's response to being incarcerated at the Minidoka concentration camp in Idaho in 1942 with his wife Marion and his six-week-old daughter Mira. The line is delivered without bitterness and is the documentary's clearest piece of family ethics. Marion Nakashima, Mira reports separately, was "not so happy with the situation." George — having spent three years inside Sri Aurobindo's ashram — applied the discipline he had learned in Pondicherry to the Idaho desert. He app

key_quotes.quote: After a while, he said chickens and he were not psychologically compatible.
key_quotes.context: Mira is delivering one of the most-quoted lines in the entire history of American craft. After Antonin Raymond sponsored the Nakashimas out of Minidoka in 1943, Raymond required George to work as a chicken farmer on his Bucks County, Pennsylvania farm. The architecture firm couldn't legally employ Nakashima — the practice was working on government war projects and could not employ a recently-incarcerated Japanese American on those contracts. So Nakashima farmed chickens. The dryness of the line

key_quotes.quote: There was a sculptor in Japan named Nagare, and he said that you couldn't have evil thoughts in a Nakashima chair. It put you in a certain state. So in a sense for George, a chair is like a throne.
key_quotes.context: Mira is quoting Masayuki Nagare (1923-2018), the Japanese sculptor whose 1964 "Stone Crazy" wall stood at the New York World's Fair and who became close friends with George Nakashima. Nakashima and Nagare met when Nagare came to New Hope for steak dinner with his stonemasons after the World's Fair commission. George Nakashima had traveled to England specifically to acquire logs from the country's diminishing reserves of English oak burl and English walnut — both species he prized. The Arts Building was designed in 1967 specifically

key_quotes.context: Mira is showing the camera a small three-legged wooden chair her father designed and built specifically for her around 1952, when she was ten years old. The Mira Chair is now a recognized piece in the Nakashima Studio catalog. It is also a piece of family record. key_quotes.quote: It's also respecting the material. People nowadays cut down thousands of acres of trees and there's so much material that's wasted, and dad did not want to waste material.
key_quotes.context: Mira closes the documentary with the working philosophy her father carried from Sri Aurobindo's Pondicherry ashram, through the Idaho concentration camp, into the New Hope workshop, and across forty-seven years of running a furniture business that refused industrial mass production. The line — "respecting the material" — is the secular translation of the Sundarananda name. Treating wood as a substance that has earned the right to keep its character. us0xeBVKMJI.title: Jason Bateman's Closet Picks
us0xeBVKMJI.synopsis: Jason Bateman visits the Criterion Closet shortly before the September 2025 launch of *Black Rabbit*, the eight-episode Netflix limited series he co-stars in opposite Jude Law and directs the first two episodes of. The picks split cleanly into three groups: films Bateman is embarrassed to have never seen (*Amores Perros*, *Moonstruck*, the Three Colors trilogy), films that directly shape his work as a director (*Klute*, *All the President's Men*, *Barry Lyndon*, *Funny Games*), and a closing trio of comedies that demonstrate the kind of acting he reveres (*Being There*, *Shampoo*). The through-line is cinematographer Gordon Willis. Bateman names Willis explicitly as the master of low-light photography whose work on *Klute* and *All the President's Men* informs the dark, contrasty visual style he developed across *Ozark* and brought to *Black Rabbit* — to the point that he tells viewers complaining about how dark his shows look on small screens to send their letters to Willis. The video also surfaces a personal note that does not appear in most Bateman interviews: his father told him about the Three Colors trilogy and inspired him to go into both acting and directing in the first place. The closing sequence on Hal Ashby and Peter Sellers — what Bateman calls no-winking comedy, where the characters would be deeply offended if they ever heard anyone laughing at them — articulates a comedic principle that explains a great deal about his own performances across *Arrested Development*, *Ozark*, *Carry-On*, and now *Black Rabbit*.

key_quotes.quote: A bunch of shame all the way through this Closet.
key_quotes.context: Bateman's framing of the entire segment after admitting he has never seen *Amores Perros* or *Moonstruck*. The line establishes the structural device the rest of the video runs on — every pick is either something he has not seen and needs to, or something he has seen and reveres for a specific technical reason.

key_quotes.quote: My father always told me about these films, as something that I need to see and should see. He's the one that kind of inspired me to go into acting and directing, and he said that I need to see these films.
key_quotes.context: Picking the Three Colors trilogy. The most personally revealing moment in the segment — a disclosure that his father Kent Bateman, an actor and director himself, is the reason Jason went into both acting and directing in the first place, and that the Kieślowski films were on a recommendation list he still has not worked through.

key_quotes.quote: This guy loves to, you know, keep the fill side real dark. He's just a master at low-light photography, was, and is a big inspiration for me.
key_quotes.context: Picking *Klute* and naming Gordon Willis. The clearest available statement of Bateman's debt to a specific cinematographer — a debt that defines the visual style of *Ozark* and *Black Rabbit*.

key_quotes.quote: It's his fault. Send your letters to him.
key_quotes.context: After joking that viewers should watch his shows on bigger screens at night because they are so dark. The half-joke contains a serious working argument that the dark visual style of his prestige drama is a deliberate inheritance from Willis, not an accident.

key_quotes.quote: That's the comfortable part for me, and so it's not the exciting part for me. The exciting part for me is camera.
key_quotes.context: Distinguishing between working with actors — actor-talk — and operating the camera. The cleanest articulation of why an actor who has been working since childhood became a director: not for the actors, but for the frame.

key_quotes.quote: I didn't get to go to film school. Thank God Criterion's around, you know?
key_quotes.context: After picking *Barry Lyndon* and noting the candlelight cinematography and the special lenses Kubrick had made for the production. The line places the entire Closet segment in the context of Bateman's autodidact career — a working television actor who learned directing on the job and is using physical media as continuing education.

key_quotes.quote: There's a way that Europeans film violence and danger that I admire. I haven't seen enough of it.
key_quotes.context: Picking *Funny Games* and explaining why. The most consequential single sentence in the video for understanding the tonal target of *Black Rabbit*, which released three months after this segment with Bateman directing the first two episodes and playing the gambling-addict brother whose return triggers the criminal violence.

key_quotes.quote: It's just characters that would be deeply offended if they ever heard anybody laughing at what they were going through and what they were doing or saying. Takes a lot of good acting to do this kind of comedy.
key_quotes.context: Defining what he calls no-winking comedy and crediting Peter Sellers as the original practitioner and Hal Ashby as the director who built films around the technique. The principle explains the comedic logic of Bateman's own performance work across *Arrested Development*, *Ozark*, and *Carry-On* — characters whose sincerity is the source of the humor.

ErwYl1eU3is.title: Jon Hamm’s Closet Picks
ErwYl1eU3is.synopsis: Jon Hamm enters the Criterion Closet and admits the obvious problem within thirty-five milliseconds of walking in: he can already see three hundred movies he wants to discuss. He works through ten picks anyway. He opens with Greg Mottola's *The Daytrippers*, anchoring his first choice to a personal friendship — Mottola directed Hamm in *Confess, Fletch*. He moves to Pixar's *WALL·E*, calling it a brilliant near-silent piece of storytelling that makes him cry every time. He chooses Lasse Hallström's *My Life as a Dog* for its breathtaking heartbreak, then turns hard into weirdness with David Cronenberg's *Videodrome*, picked from Criterion Channel's "VHS Forever" themed collection. Hamm explicitly connects Bruce Robinson's *How to Get Ahead in Advertising* to his decade as Don Draper on *Mad Men*, praising the title's pun and Robinson's surreal voice. The picks darken with George Sluizer's *The Vanishing* — one of the scariest movies he has ever seen — and the Belgian satire *Man Bites Dog*, which he describes as prescient about media manipulation and reality TV before either existed in their modern forms. He selects Jim Jarmusch's *Down by Law* as a vibey gateway to the director who picked up the John Cassavetes mantle, and calls Sydney Pollack's *Tootsie* a perfect comedy because it stays deeply funny while remaining genuinely heartbreaking. He closes with Wes Anderson's *Bottle Rocket*, the debut featuring all three Wilson brothers, holding it up as proof that aspiring filmmakers need a fun story and talented people more than money.

key_quotes.quote: I immediately saw 300 movies that I wanted to talk about. But I will attempt to be brief... ish.
key_quotes.context: Hamm's opening admission about the impossibility of the assignment. The line sets the tone for the visit and hangs a lantern on the structural problem of the format — every Criterion Closet guest discovers that walking into the room turns ten picks into three hundred — and Hamm's pause before "ish" is the first laugh of the video.

key_quotes.quote: It is a brilliant piece of storytelling, almost silent movie. It is so well told.
key_quotes.context: Hamm's verdict on *WALL·E* delivered in his most direct register. He is making a craft argument — that an animated movie containing almost no dialogue can carry as much emotional and narrative weight as any live-action film — and he closes the same beat by saying the movie makes him cry every time he sees it.

key_quotes.quote: This is kind of a weird one: Videodrome, David Cronenberg. A crazy Canadian genius.
key_quotes.context: Hamm's introduction to his most challenging pick. Calling Cronenberg "a crazy Canadian genius" telegraphs the body-horror filmography that *Videodrome* sits inside — *The Fly*, *Dead Ringers*, *Crash*, *eXistenZ* — and signals to the audience that the next pick is going to feel different from a Pixar movie or a Hallström coming-of-age film.

key_quotes.quote: The title is a pun. I love a pun in a title... It's about a guy who grows a head that has really interesting opinions.
key_quotes.context: Hamm explaining the appeal of *How to Get Ahead in Advertising*. The "interesting opinions" line is the pull quote — it captures Bruce Robinson's premise without spoiling the satire — and the pun fixation gives a glimpse of how Hamm thinks about the connection between language and material.

key_quotes.quote: This movie, this is maybe one of the scariest movies I've ever seen.
key_quotes.context: Hamm's introduction to *The Vanishing*. The pause before the sentence and the qualifier "maybe" are doing real work — Hamm is an actor who has been around enough horror movies to grade them, and he is putting Sluizer's Dutch original near the top of the list. He follows the line with a plot summary that explains exactly what makes the film disturbing.

key_quotes.quote: I hate to keep using this word prescient, but this is about media and manipulation.
key_quotes.context: Hamm flagging his own repetition. He has just used "prescient" for *WALL·E* and is now using it again for *Man Bites Dog*, and the self-awareness is the point — three of his picks are films that saw the present moment coming, and he wants the audience to notice the pattern.

key_quotes.quote: Super vibey and definitely picks up the mantle of people like John Cassavetes and all of these incredible independent filmmakers.
key_quotes.context: Hamm placing Jim Jarmusch in the American independent lineage. The Cassavetes name-check is the deepest cinephile move of the visit — Cassavetes is the patron saint of American indie filmmaking — and Hamm is arguing that Jarmusch is the artist who carried that tradition forward into the 80s and 90s.

key_quotes.quote: I think, and I'm not the only person to say this, a perfect comedy. Deeply funny. Very, very heartbreaking.
key_quotes.context: Hamm's verdict on *Tootsie*. The phrase "I'm not the only person to say this" is Hamm acknowledging the consensus position he is joining — *Tootsie* has been called one of the great American comedies for forty years — and the deeply-funny-very-heartbreaking pairing is the cleanest definition of his comedic standard in the entire video.

key_quotes.quote: This is a great movie for aspiring filmmakers to look at because it proves you just need a fun story and talented people to make a great movie.
key_quotes.context: Hamm's closing line about *Bottle Rocket*. He is making a direct address to anyone in the audience trying to make a film, and he uses Wes Anderson and the Wilson brothers as proof of concept. The fact that the Criterion Closet visit ends on a piece of advice rather than another pick is the visit's moral.
