aPY7bhPj_08.title: Josh Safdie & Sean Baker | The A24 Podcast
aPY7bhPj_08.content_type: Director-to-Director Podcast / Marty Supreme Behind-the-Scenes Conversation
aPY7bhPj_08.synopsis: Sean Baker, fresh off the Best Picture Oscar for Anora, sits down with Josh Safdie — making his first solo directing feature without his brother Benny since the Safdie Brothers years that produced Daddy Longlegs, Heaven Knows What, Good Time, and Uncut Gems — to take Marty Supreme apart from the inside. Sean opens by reading back a 2020 text exchange in which Josh first told him he was "falling more and more into maximalist territory" — a phrase Sean did not understand at the time and now recognizes as the working theory of Marty Supreme. Josh walks Sean through the operating philosophy: looking at life as a construct, recreating it from scratch with overwhelming specificity, and then "stealing the movie" from inside the construct as if it were a documentary. The conversation goes deep on production design — Jack Fisk and set decorator Adam Willis approached the 1950s Lower East Side as time travel rather than nostalgia, excavating a real garment shop on Orchard Street down to the bones of the original company; the Tree of Life-era Malick anecdote where Jack Fisk personally moved a 25-foot-diameter root ball oak tree across location; the Bausch & Lomb CinemaScope lenses Fox commissioned in 1953 that survive in only two sets in the world today, plus a NASA-designed Vivitar f/0.8 lens Josh used in low-light scenes that Stanley Kubrick had reportedly used on Barry Lyndon. Casting director Jennifer Venditti's "deep dive spiritual" twenty-minute interviews become character rewrites; cinematographer Darius Khondji ("the Prince of Darkness") tolerates Josh's refusal to put marks on the floor because the imperfection of an actor slightly underlit is more interesting than precision; Sean reveals he edits Anora and Red Rocket "100% high"; and the conversation on writing-versus-direction-versus-editing produces the working theory that writing is the hardest part because it could go on forever. The most cinematically vivid casting story in the conversation is Josh going through the Forbes top 50 richest people list, finding none of them cinematic enough, eventually arriving at Shark Tank's Kevin O'Leary as the corporate-colonialist antagonist, putting himself on a Zoom pitch like he was on the show, and getting from O'Leary the improvised line — "Marty, I was born in 1601, I'm a vampire" — that almost became the film's literal ending at a 1987 Tears for Fears concert. The two directors close by triangulating the same working theory across both their careers: their early treatments-rather-than-scripts (Prince of Broadway, Daddy Longlegs) were thirst for reality; their current films (Anora, Marty Supreme) are thirst for story; and the technical achievement of Marty Supreme is making a $50,000-feeling stolen-from-life movie out of a major-studio period production.

key_quotes.quote: I'm falling more and more into maximalist territory.
key_quotes.context: The defining sentence of the entire podcast, written by Josh in October 2020 in a text exchange with Sean Baker about the difficulty of keeping films small. Sean reads the sentence back at [02:43] and admits that at the time he received it, he thought "what the hell is he talking about." Five years later, watching Marty Supreme three times, Sean understands the sentence as the working program that produces the film. The phrase carries the conceptual load of Josh's whole current method. Maximalis

key_quotes.quote: How can I create life to have the feeling of going inside of it and stealing the movie because that is like the urgency of like getting something and getting out.
key_quotes.context: The single sentence in which Josh's whole working method is most efficiently captured. The grammatical structure — create life, then enter, then steal — describes the exact maneuver that distinguishes Marty Supreme from a conventional period production. A conventional period production designs a set, populates it with actors, and shoots the planned shots. Josh's method creates a recreated reality at the level of comprehensiveness where it can be approached the way Heaven Knows What approached th

key_quotes.quote: It's amazing cuz in that moment you're watching like two real people. A guy sneezes. That's the most real you can get.
key_quotes.context: Josh's specific identification of a sneeze in Sean Baker's 2008 Prince of Broadway as the moment that exemplifies what realist cinema can capture. The sneeze is not scripted; the response of the other character to the sneeze is not scripted; the decision to "remain like hard a little bit" rather than say bless you is a micro-emotional choice an actor cannot fake. The reason Josh keeps returning to the sneeze is that it sets the floor for what reality-stealing has to produce. Marty Supreme has to

key_quotes.quote: We had this real life document of what that street was like. So, that was like the standard. That was it was life.
key_quotes.context: Josh's framing of how Ken Jacobs's 1955 short film Orchard Street functioned for the production team as the operational reference document. Jacobs's son Azazel Jacobs had restored the film. Josh's whole production used it as "the Bible" for what the actual Lower East Side looked, sounded, and moved like in 1949-1955. The structural argument the line is making is about the difference between research and reference. Most period productions do research — books, photographs, costume reference, archi

key_quotes.quote: Underneath it all was the bones of this original kind of, you know, this clothing company. And when we excavated, we had to we had to completely clear it out.
key_quotes.context: Josh's compressed account of the Orchard Street shop excavation. The literal excavation language is the right register because the production was performing actual archaeology — clearing out decades of accumulated "garment slop" sitting on top of the building's original 1940s architecture. The "bones of this original kind of clothing company" carries the same archaeological metaphor: the building's original purpose was structurally still present beneath the layers of subsequent commercial life.

key_quotes.quote: It has a timeless quality. has an aura. Like look at that, look that's so period. And the way they said that, I don't know, I always really, it was very spiritual to me. The art department.
key_quotes.context: Josh's account of how Jack Fisk and Adam Willis's working vocabulary distinguished period reality from period imitation. Their phrase "that's so period" did not mean what audiences think the phrase means. They were not pointing at period-appropriate objects and approving them as adequately retro. They were pointing at objects that had taken on what Josh hears as "a timeless thing in front of us... an aura." The principle Fisk and Willis are working from is that period quality is not a function o

key_quotes.quote: Jen's like a humanist. And I'm lucky she's like — she's a documentarian. Her movie Billy the Kid is a testament to amazing movie.
key_quotes.context: Josh's framing of Jennifer Venditti's working philosophy as humanist in a precise sense. Humanism here is operational rather than philosophical — Venditti looks at every potential cast member, regardless of professional credit, as a real person whose actual life is the source of what they can bring to a character. The Billy the Kid reference at [19:53] is the working evidence. Venditti's 2007 documentary follows a single autistic teenager in small-town Maine as he negotiates a first crush — a fi

key_quotes.quote: Her interviews are amazing. They're like deep dive spiritual.
key_quotes.context: Josh's working description of Jennifer Venditti's twenty-minute casting interview process. The "deep dive spiritual" phrase is the operational description of what the interviews are actually doing. They are not auditions; they do not test the actor's ability to deliver the existing scene. They are conversations that go past the scene into the actual life of the person being interviewed, and Josh receives the interviews as research material that he writes back into the script. The actor then watc

key_quotes.quote: Well, this movie feels very nostalgic and it would be interesting to kind of at least evoke the Twin Towers.
key_quotes.context: Josh's recounting of what financiers asked him to add to what would become Daddy Longlegs. The film took place on a beach. It did not take place on or near September 11. The lawyers offering one hundred thousand dollars in financing wanted "the Twin Towers" evoked because the film "felt very nostalgic." The reason the line works as a quotable moment in a 2026 podcast is that it captures the absurd specificity of the conditions under which young filmmakers are asked to compromise their material.

key_quotes.quote: The caveman around the fire hearing a middle and end.
key_quotes.context: Josh's compressed articulation of the deep-historical theory of why both his and Sean Baker's careers have moved toward more traditional story structure. The caveman-around-the-fire image identifies story not as a competitor to reality but as the older form of communicating reality. A circle of listeners around a fire receiving a beginning-middle-end is the original cinema, and the apparatus of films built on traditional story arcs is downstream of an oral tradition older than written language.

NnldiqFIStY.title: Lucy Liu's Closet Picks
NnldiqFIStY.synopsis: Lucy Liu visits the Criterion Collection closet and pulls down six items across three minutes and thirty-six seconds. The picks read as a working actress's lineage diagram. Two are Hong Kong martial-arts icons who cleared the runway for her career — the Jackie Chan release she pulls is for the man who refused to teach her stunts on Shanghai Noon (2000) because he had broken every bone in his body, and the Bruce Lee Criterion box set is for the artist who, as Liu puts it, "started Hollywood, in a way, for Asian Americans" before his sudden death at thirty-two. One is contemporary cinema mastery: Alfonso Cuarón's Roma left her "completely blown away" — she calls it "cinema," full stop. Two are performance studies: Marlon Brando in Elia Kazan's On the Waterfront, the film Liu watched when she first started acting thirty years ago and identifies as her first true definition of authenticity, and Stephen Frears's The Grifters, where Anjelica Huston, Annette Bening, and John Cusack "punched a bunch of holes in my body" with their delicious character work. The closing pick is a random pull with eyes closed: Wong Kar-wai's In the Mood for Love, which Liu identifies as the universal visual template for every mood board, photography deck, and cover shoot in the creative industry. The visit closes with Liu thanking the spirit. The picks weren't random. The lineage is hers.

key_quotes.quote: He said that he shouldn't teach me stunts because he broke every bone in his body.
key_quotes.context: Liu is recalling her on-set conversation with Jackie Chan during the production of Tom Dey's 2000 Shanghai Noon, in which Liu played Princess Pei-Pei opposite Chan's Imperial Guard Chon Wang. Liu had asked Chan, then forty-six, to teach her stunts. The "every bone in his body" line is delivered as exact recall — Chan really has documented dozens of fractures across his career, including a near-fatal skull fracture from a fall on the Armour of God set in 1986 that left him with a permanent hole i

key_quotes.quote: And this is cinema.
key_quotes.context: Liu is closing her pick of Alfonso Cuarón's 2018 Roma, the black-and-white Mexico City childhood memoir that won three Academy Awards including Best Director and Best Cinematography. The line lands as a verdict. Liu has spent thirty years inside an industry that has gradually traded the older definition of cinema (theatrical, photochemical, director-driven, slow) for the newer one (streaming, content-volume, franchise-driven, fast). Roma was Netflix-distributed and streaming-first, which would s

key_quotes.quote: Bruce Lee is somebody that had started Hollywood, in a way, for Asian Americans.
key_quotes.context: Liu is opening her Bruce Lee pick — almost certainly the Criterion Bruce Lee: His Greatest Hits box set released in July 2020. The "in a way" qualifier is doing the careful historical work. Anna May Wong was on screen in Hollywood productions in the 1920s and 1930s and received a posthumous Walk of Fame star in 1960 (Liu became only the second Asian American woman to receive that star, in 2019). Sessue Hayakawa was a Hollywood lead in the 1910s and 1920s. Bruce Lee's specific contribution was ma

key_quotes.quote: I really understood what authenticity was from this movie.
key_quotes.context: Liu is talking about Marlon Brando's performance as longshoreman Terry Malloy in Elia Kazan's 1954 On the Waterfront. The "really understood" framing is the working actress's way of saying she had read about Method acting, watched other Brando films, and intellectually grasped the concept of authenticity before — but On the Waterfront was the moment the concept became operational for her own work. Liu situates the moment "over thirty years ago," placing it around 1993 to 1995, when she was worki

key_quotes.quote: It just punched a bunch of holes in my body.
key_quotes.context: Liu is describing what watching The Grifters did to her. The image is physical and specific. Performances by Anjelica Huston (as long-game grifter Lilly Dillon), Annette Bening (as Myra Langtry), and John Cusack (as Roy Dillon, Lilly's son and Myra's lover) operated, for Liu, as something like punctures — moments of recognition that left her unable to rebuild the same defensive surface afterward. The Grifters earned all three actors major recognition (Huston and Bening were Academy Award nominee

key_quotes.quote: He created something that has become what people use as their template.
key_quotes.context: Liu is closing her In the Mood for Love pick with the industry observation that Wong Kar-wai's 2000 film has become the universal mood-board reference across the Hollywood and fashion industries. The "template" word is the operative one. Liu has been on the receiving end of decks, mood boards, and cover-shoot reference packages for twenty-five years — as an actress preparing for roles, as a producer evaluating projects, as a Vogue and Vanity Fair cover subject, as a director and visual artist. I

Ddh6FBvHFi0.title: Margaret Qualley's Closet Picks
Ddh6FBvHFi0.synopsis: Margaret Qualley spends two minutes and twenty-five seconds in the Criterion Closet pulling six movies that map the territory between her dance training and her acting career. She opens with Claire Denis's Beau travail and singles out Denis Lavant's closing dance sequence — Qualley says she is "obsessed" with him — without mentioning that she herself starred in a Claire Denis film called Stars at Noon. She moves through Gus Van Sant's To Die For, quoting Nicole Kidman's "bringing the world into our homes" monologue from memory and calling Kidman's performance "perfectly psychotic" [01:05]. She quotes Adam Sandler's Mattress Man speech from Punch-Drunk Love verbatim and uses the moment to argue that Happy Gilmore and Big Daddy belong on Criterion shelves [01:21]. She declares Bob Fosse's All That Jazz the greatest movie ever made and says she would want to dance with Fosse rather than have dinner with him [02:09]. She closes with Ridley Scott's Thelma & Louise and states outright that she wants to make a female road-trip movie of her own [02:21]. Five seconds on All About Eve in the middle. Throughout, dance is the throughline — Qualley's two strongest endorsements both center on movement, and the entire visit reads as a working-actor's notebook of physical performance.

key_quotes.quote: I love my girl Claire.
key_quotes.context: Qualley is referring to Claire Denis, the French director of Beau travail, and the casual possessive "my girl Claire" is doing more work than it appears to. Most actors discussing a director of Denis's stature in a Criterion-sponsored video would use the formal name and the formal register. Qualley uses the register a person uses for a friend or a collaborator, which is what she actually is — Qualley starred in Denis's Stars at Noon (2022), which premiered at Cannes and won the Grand Prix. The l

key_quotes.quote: Denis Lavant, who I'm, like, obsessed with.
key_quotes.context: Qualley names the lead actor of Beau travail and immediately admits a level of fandom that goes well beyond the casual viewer's appreciation. Lavant's career sits almost entirely inside the films of Leos Carax — Boy Meets Girl (1984), Mauvais Sang (1986), The Lovers on the Bridge (1991), Holy Motors (2012), and Annette (2021) — with Beau travail as the major outside-Carax exception. To be obsessed with Lavant is to be obsessed with a particular kind of male physical performance: small body, athl

key_quotes.quote: Perfectly psychotic.
key_quotes.context: Qualley's two-word judgment of Nicole Kidman's performance as Suzanne Stone in Gus Van Sant's To Die For. The compression is the achievement — most acting criticism takes a paragraph to say what Qualley says in two words. "Perfectly" names Kidman's craft: total, technically immaculate commitment to the character's sincerity. "Psychotic" names what that commitment reveals: Suzanne Stone is empty inside, a manipulator whose surface conviction conceals nothing because there's nothing underneath to

key_quotes.quote: I would name Happy Gilmore or Big Daddy if they were in this closet, and they should be in this closet, I think.
key_quotes.context: The most genre-busting line in the video. Qualley is making a curatorial argument the Criterion Collection has no obvious mechanism for absorbing — that Dennis Dugan's Adam Sandler comedies belong on the same shelves as Bergman, Kurosawa, and Tarkovsky. The argument has more rigor than it appears to. Qualley is implicitly challenging the Collection's curatorial logic, which privileges films that reward repeated rigorous viewing — the kind of film you study, not the kind of film you live with. Ha

key_quotes.quote: I would say that's that, Mr. Mattress Man.
key_quotes.attribution: Margaret Qualley quoting Adam Sandler in Punch-Drunk Love
key_quotes.context: The closing line of Barry Egan's confrontation scene with Dean Trumbell — the bullying phone-sex-line operator played by Philip Seymour Hoffman. Qualley quotes the speech from memory because it is the emotional pivot of Punch-Drunk Love, the moment Sandler's character finally claims his own strength against the cycle of humiliation that has defined his life. The line works because of what comes before it in the speech: "I have a love in my life that makes me stronger than anything you could ever

key_quotes.quote: I think this is the greatest movie ever made.
key_quotes.context: Qualley is talking about Bob Fosse's All That Jazz (1979), and the absolute claim is unusual for an actor performing cinephilia in a Criterion-sponsored format. Most Closet Picks subjects hedge their canonical claims; Qualley does not. All That Jazz is Fosse's autobiographical movie about a workaholic choreographer-director (Roy Scheider as Joe Gideon, Fosse's stand-in) who works himself to death while editing a movie, choreographing a Broadway show, and burning through romantic relationships. I

key_quotes.quote: Bob Fosse, except I wouldn't want to go to dinner. I'd just want to dance.
key_quotes.context: Qualley is rejecting the standard celebrity-interview question — "If you could have dinner with someone..." — by changing the medium of the imagined encounter. She doesn't want Fosse the conversationalist. She wants Fosse the body. This is the most explicit moment in the video where her ballet training surfaces as the operating principle behind every pick. The dance partner Qualley is imagining isn't a fantasy — Fosse died in 1987, Qualley was born in 1994, the only meeting between them was alwa

key_quotes.quote: I'd love to make a movie like this.
key_quotes.context: The visit's most concrete career disclosure. Qualley is holding Thelma & Louise and stating, on camera, in a Criterion-sponsored video, that she wants to make a female road-trip movie of her own. The line is unusually direct for the format. Closet Picks is designed to extract admiration, not ambition; most subjects describe what a film means to them and stop. Qualley names a project. The female-led American road movie is a small and specific lineage — Barbara Loden's Wanda (1970), Agnès Varda's

HuHd2ssPrGg.title: Mary Bronstein & Conan O'Brien | A24 x Letterboxd: The List
HuHd2ssPrGg.synopsis: Mary Bronstein and Conan O'Brien sit down for A24's Letterboxd "The List" segment, paired here because Conan plays Linda's therapist in Bronstein's *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You* — the 2025 A24 release directed by Bronstein and starring Rose Byrne in an Oscar-nominated performance. The pairing is structurally pointed: Bronstein is the writer-director who quit filmmaking for seventeen years between *Yeast* (2008) and *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You* (2025), and Conan O'Brien is the late-night legend whose dramatic acting debut in this very film is the project the conversation is promoting. The segment runs only 4:40 but is unusually dense with cinephile disclosures. Conan's most recent professional context matters here — he hosted the 97th Academy Awards in March 2025 to massive acclaim, was immediately re-booked for the 98th Oscars in March 2026, voices Smartypants in *Toy Story 5* (Pixar, June 2026), continues his HBO/Max travel series *Conan O'Brien Must Go* and his podcast *Conan O'Brien Needs a Friend*, and turned in a restrained dramatic performance in Bronstein's film that Mary Bronstein herself has publicly called one of her greatest pleasures of the production. The conversation rotates through Letterboxd top-four prompts at speed. Bronstein opens with *Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles* (1990) as her first-in-theaters film — her father fell asleep next to her during one of the loudest movies ever made. Conan trumps her with the joke that he watched his own sonogram in utero, then names *Mary Poppins* and Dick Van Dyke dancing as the chimney sweep on the rooftop as the moment he decided he wanted to grow up to be "a tall lanky person who dances around as a chimney sweep" — a vocational prediction that, he notes drily, did come true. The two name *That's Entertainment* (the 1974 MGM compilation) as a shared touchstone (Conan keeps the poster in his bedroom), introduce Conan's signature critical framework "Dude needs to chill" applied to *Ordinary People*, *Doctor Zhivago*, and *Taxi Driver*, trade stress-cinema picks (*Uncut Gems* — written by Bronstein's husband Ronald Bronstein with Josh Safdie — versus *Midsommar*), and confess formative childhood horror traumas (Conan watching The Exorcist on broadcast TV at nine, Bronstein scarred by an unannounced *Night of the Living Dead* trailer at the Hearthstone Plaza in Brookline). Bronstein closes with her canon of women writer-directors making films about women — Chantal Akerman, Barbara Loden, Ida Lupino — and Conan caps the segment with a riff about a hypothetical career list of "movies Conan O'Brien's in playing a dramatic role" containing only *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You*, plus the surrealist pitch of inserting his face into Clint Eastwood's *Every Which Way But Loose*.

key_quotes.quote: I want to be a tall lanky person who dances around as a chimney sweep. And in a way... it did come true.
key_quotes.context: Conan is articulating the foundational vocational moment of his entire career — watching Dick Van Dyke's chimney sweep Bert dance on the rooftops in *Mary Poppins* (1964) at age four or five, and deciding in the theater that the chimney sweep was the future self he wanted to inhabit. The disclosure is the segment's most accidentally autobiographical moment, and the most precise possible articulation of how a single childhood film can imprint a vocational template onto a viewer who has not yet co

key_quotes.quote: That's what I got to do. I just want to put on a big show for everybody and I want it shot in beautiful sort of late '30s, early '40s color and I want it to be shown to people who are just about to fight World War II.
key_quotes.context: Conan is recounting the moment he watched Jack Haley Jr.'s 1974 MGM compilation *That's Entertainment!* as a "little orange-haired kid" and decided cinema was his career. The temporal compression in the disclosure is the precise comic mechanism. Conan, watching in 1974, was responding to footage of MGM musicals from the 1930s and 1940s — and his five-year-old wish was not for a contemporary 1974 audience but specifically for an audience "just about to fight World War II." The wish is impossible,

key_quotes.quote: Eraserhead. It's a radical expression of a very male anxiety of having a child and not being sure if you want to have that child and only the way that a man can be anxious about it.
key_quotes.context: Bronstein is articulating the most analytically pointed reading of any film in the segment. David Lynch's 1977 *Eraserhead* — the surreal industrial-nightmare film about a young man trapped with a deformed infant — has been read by film critics for forty-eight years as a metaphor for paternal anxiety, but Bronstein's specific framing locates the reading inside a gendered phenomenological claim: that the film could only have been made by a man because the anxiety it dramatizes is structurally mal

key_quotes.quote: Ordinary People. Kid needs to learn to chill and know it's not his fault.
key_quotes.context: Conan introduces the conversation's most analytically interesting comic framework with Robert Redford's 1980 *Ordinary People* — the family-grief drama in which Timothy Hutton's surviving brother is consumed by guilt over his older brother's accidental drowning. The "Dude needs to chill" framework that Conan is about to extend across multiple prestige dramas is doing more work than it appears. The framework is, on the surface, comic reductiveness — the suggestion that complex psychological drama

key_quotes.quote: Doctor Zhivago would be, 'Dude needs to chill and just write his poems.' You know, Taxi Driver, 'Dude needs to chill and yeah, and just get some sleep and drive that cab.' Across the board, 'Dude needs to chill.'
key_quotes.context: Conan extends the "Dude needs to chill" framework across two of the most acclaimed dramatic films in cinema history — David Lean's 1965 *Doctor Zhivago* (the six-Oscar-winning Russian Revolution romantic epic with Omar Sharif and Julie Christie) and Martin Scorsese's 1976 *Taxi Driver* (the Robert De Niro psychological-violence street drama that defined a particular post-Vietnam American masculinity). The reductions are doing precise comic work. *Doctor Zhivago*'s entire 197-minute runtime is st

key_quotes.quote: I sat there and they were right. It was incredibly stressful.
key_quotes.context: Bronstein is describing her experience of finally watching *Uncut Gems* — the Safdie Brothers' 2019 Adam Sandler diamond-district-anxiety masterpiece written and edited by her husband Ronald Bronstein with Josh Safdie — for the first time at the public premiere, after deliberately refusing to see a frame of it during production. The disclosure is doing precise working-spouse-of-a-filmmaker biographical work. Most spouses of working filmmakers see early cuts as part of the home-office bleed of th

key_quotes.quote: There's not enough food and that upsets me because I come from a large family and if I had sat there with my brothers and sisters and they just put out some hazelnuts and some grapes, I'd have started killing people.
key_quotes.context: Conan is articulating his unexpected stress-cinema response to Ari Aster's 2019 *Midsommar* — not the violence, the cult ritual, or the slow-motion psychological collapse of Florence Pugh's Dani, but the food. The disclosure is the segment's funniest moment and is also accidentally doing precise film criticism. The spare table-setting in *Midsommar* is, in fact, a deliberate visual choice. Aster and his production designer Henrik Svensson built the Hårga commune's communal-meal aesthetic around

key_quotes.quote: I would stay up late to see the Honeymooners free runs and then right after that they were like, 'The movie of the night, The Exorcist.' And I think I was nine or 10 and it terrified me so badly even the edited for television version.
key_quotes.context: Conan is articulating the specific generational television-childhood-trauma experience of his pre-cable broadcast viewing, in which an R-rated horror film could be edited down for late-night network broadcast and aired immediately after a 1955 black-and-white Jackie Gleason sitcom. The temporal compression is precise. *The Honeymooners* (1955-1956) was already a syndicated rerun by the time Conan was nine or ten in 1972-1973. *The Exorcist* (1973) had just had its theatrical release. The broadca

key_quotes.quote: I was at the Hearthstone Plaza seeing a movie when I was also very young and it was a kid-friendly movie, but they showed trailers for what was coming up and they showed a trailer for Night of the Living Dead.
key_quotes.context: Conan is describing his second formative-horror experience at the Hearthstone Plaza — the Brookline, Massachusetts movie theater complex where he grew up. The theater, originally a Walter Reade venue called the Plaza, opened in 1971 and closed by the early 1990s; the building is now part of the New England Institute of Art. 9AUc5BfebCM.title: Mikey Madison’s Closet Picks
9AUc5BfebCM.synopsis: Mikey Madison visits the Criterion Closet for a brief, four-minute-and-fifty-four-second selection of films she wants to take home, and uses the occasion to articulate the casting principle behind her career: she's drawn to morally gray characters, the kind of role Jack Nicholson plays in Bob Rafelson's Five Easy Pieces. The selection moves through the New Hollywood era (Five Easy Pieces, Easy Rider) into European art cinema (The Piano Teacher, Eyes Without a Face), Old Hollywood iconography (Gilda), early independent film (Sean Baker's Take Out), silent-era classics (Charlie Chaplin's The Kid), and a final blind pull from the shelf that lands on Michael Cimino's revisionist Western Heaven's Gate. The most personally revealing moment is Madison's discovery, mid-Closet, of a film whose costar is the namesake of the legal account that protected her own teenage earnings — Jackie Coogan, whose parents' theft of his The Kid earnings led to California's 1939 Child Actor's Bill. Throughout, Madison treats Isabelle Huppert's performance in The Piano Teacher as her standard of acting greatness, returning to Huppert again at the end when she pulls Heaven's Gate from the shelf and reads its cast list. Her praise for Dennis Hopper's behind-the-scenes Easy Rider photography, which she keeps in book form on her coffee table, doubles as a description of her own working method on movie sets.

Om6PPF-C_G4.title: Mitski’s Closet Picks
Om6PPF-C_G4.synopsis: Mitski visits the Criterion Collection closet and pulls down five films that map her formative cinephile education. The picks are unusually articulate. Daisies (Věra Chytilová's 1966 Czech New Wave anarchy film) was Mitski's accidental discovery as a seventh-grader at a video rental store run by "pale nerds" in their twenties with bangs over their eyes — the year that turned her into a cinephile. Moonstruck (Norman Jewison, 1987) was the start of her Nicolas Cage fandom and the source of her own coinage "dream acting" — a Cage performance theory that prizes the actor's projection of inner emotional life over realism. The Breakfast Club (John Hughes, 1985) was the VHS Mitski watched before first grade — too young to understand "hot beef injection" but old enough to recite it. The Piano Teacher (Michael Haneke, 2001) hit her during what she calls her phase of "cold, repressed sexuality"; she walked the streets of New York "very severely" listening to Bach's Well-Tempered Clavier inhabiting Isabelle Huppert's character. My Own Private Idaho (Gus Van Sant, 1991) was Mitski's first exposure to "unconventional directing and acting" and the film that taught her movies don't have to follow rules. Mitski's first studio album Lush came out in 2012. Her eighth, Nothing's About to Happen to Me, was released in February 2026 to universal critical acclaim. The closet picks are the source code.

U--30B4mL3w.title: Nick Offerman | Good Hang with Amy Poehler
U--30B4mL3w.content_type: Podcast Interview / Parks and Recreation Reunion Conversation
U--30B4mL3w.synopsis: Amy Poehler hosts Nick Offerman on her Spotify–Ringer–Paper Kite podcast Good Hang for what she calls a long-overdue conversation between the Parks and Recreation co-stars who have somehow never publicly discussed the show together. The episode opens with Aubrey Plaza zooming in from her dressing room before going on stage in New York, reading a portion of the medieval-language birthday text chain she shares with Offerman and Jason Schwartzman because the three of them are all born on June 26, and telling the story of how Offerman corrected her on her first season of Parks and Rec when she asked a PA to bring her coffee — "Don't forget that you can get up and get your own coffee. It's right over there" — a "jolt" Plaza describes as the foundational moment of her professional disposition. The conversation then opens up onto Offerman's full body of work and current life. He has just gotten back from a UK book tour for his new children's book Little Woodchucks and is publicly entering wrestling-trainer territory for his new Apple TV+ series Margo's Got Money Troubles, in which he plays Jinx, a former pro wrestler father played opposite Elle Fanning, Nicole Kidman, and Michelle Pfeiffer — work he describes as the first time he's been asked to carry "complicated emotional relationship" rather than be the dependable bus-driver-plumber-grill-master journeyman supporting actor of his earlier years. He walks Amy through his polymath childhood in Minooka, Illinois, the miraculous hallway-loitering accident at the University of Illinois that revealed acting was a job people did, his early-90s Chicago years performing a manic-panic-dyed punk Clockwork Orange at Steppenwolf with a shaved head, and his five-month Parks and Recreation audition arc — first in for a role named Josh opposite Adam Scott reading chemistry with Rashida Jones, then back in as the eventually-cast Ron Swanson, sobbing on the phone when he got the call. He defends the mustache as a "constipative rectus" that physically alters his vocal performance toward Sam the Eagle, narrates the tipping-point moment when his wife Megan Mullally finally told him after twenty years she didn't like the facial hair, identifies the Ron-and-Tammy and Leslie-and-Ron episodes as his favorites of the run, walks through Craig Mazin offering him the Frank role on The Last of Us via their shared Offerman Wood Shop little-league dad history, plays an original mahogany ukulele song he wrote for the book about beer and whiskey and the willies of staring at phones, and answers Aubrey's planted Bill and Ted question by going to the Roman Empire, having Aubrey fight, and discovering Cumberland sausage to take over the British Isles as the Sausage King. Amy closes the episode by asking listeners to pay attention to how Ron Swanson runs in Parks and Recreation, a piece of physical comedy she identifies as a masterclass in comedic acting and a thing she has never publicly highlighted before.

BF1mmlkXSYA.title: Oscar Isaac & Ali Wong | The A24 Podcast
BF1mmlkXSYA.synopsis: This is The A24 Podcast pairing of Oscar Isaac and Ali Wong, recorded around the April 16, 2026 release of *Beef* Season 2 on Netflix. The pairing is structurally specific: Ali Wong was the Emmy-winning lead of *Beef* Season 1, and remains an executive producer on the show — but in Season 2 she does not appear on screen. The new lead is Oscar Isaac, playing Joshua Martin, the general manager of the fictional Monte Vista Point Country Club, opposite Carey Mulligan as his wife Lindsay Crane-Martin. The conversation is Wong, in her capacity as Season 1 lead and continuing executive producer, interviewing the actor who has taken over the show's anchor role from her, with the two trading craft talk and biographical detail about creator Lee Sung Jin (whose nickname is Sonny, rendered in the auto-transcript as "Sunny"). Isaac discusses being recruited by Sonny in the middle of filming Guillermo del Toro's *Frankenstein* and the two-and-a-half-hour Zoom calls of mutual vulnerability that Ali identifies as Sonny's signature creative-courtship method, often conducted while Sonny was wearing his signature Ram Dass-quote tie-dye shirts. Wong walks through what it was like for a stand-up comedian to step into a major dramatic role on Season 1, including her decision not to fight director Sonny on one-take demands she felt were impossible. Isaac and Wong analyze the Steven Yeun church-crying scene from Season 1 — the moment Yeun broke into tears under the praise music in episode three — as the canonical example of why committed actors cannot judge their own scenes as funny while playing them. Isaac talks about working with Carey Mulligan again after *Drive* (2011) and *Inside Llewyn Davis* (2013), framing their collaboration through a developmental-psychology metaphor of "parallel play" giving way to real co-creation. He details the costume-and-hair language he built with costume designer Olga Mill and hairstylist Tim Nolan — the "tiniest mullet" as a symbol of Josh's pathetic grasp at residual cool, the oversized sweaters, the Oura ring. He describes the masturbation scene, the Bufo Alvarius (5-MeO-DMT toad-venom) psychedelic sequence shot in a Korean national park at the end of the Season 2 production, and his ALS-style throat-tightening response to playing the strangled Josh. Wong closes with the story of how Sonny had originally wanted Stanley Tucci for her Season 1 role, then changed his mind during a COVID conversation and offered her the part instead. Both performers spend the final minutes reflecting on chemistry as "ease" — the rare kind of professional encounter that lets parallel play become real co-creation.

UPFnXwngSEI.title: Oscar Isaac, Carey Mulligan & Lee Sung Jin | A24 x Letterboxd: The List
UPFnXwngSEI.synopsis: Oscar Isaac, Carey Mulligan, and Lee Sung Jin sit down for A24's Letterboxd-style favorite-films roundtable and run through three minutes of rapid trade-offs that double as a portrait of how three working actors and a major showrunner actually think about cinema. Carey Mulligan opens with a defense of family films, arguing that Michael Caine's Scrooge in The Muppet Christmas Carol and Javier Bardem's title role in Lyle, Lyle, Crocodile are giving Oscar-worthy committed performances that prestige-drama actors should be measured against rather than measured above. Lee Sung Jin contributes the day's most cinematic personal anecdote — watching Park Chan-wook's The Handmaiden, one of the most erotically charged films of the 2010s, in a theater with his mother — and offers Oldboy as the film that made him realize a certain kind of transgressive cinema was even legal. Oscar Isaac runs the 80s and 90s Jean-Claude Van Damme action canon in a single breath: Bloodsport, Big Trouble in Little China, Cyborg, Starship Troopers, Kickboxer One and Two — childhood-rotation cinema where the goal of life is still to be able to do the splits and connect the groin shot in one move. Carey Mulligan offers Steven Spielberg's Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade and Jurassic Park as the engineering case for cinematic mastery — every beat exactly where it should be, endlessly rewatchable. Lee Sung Jin makes the case for Step Brothers, John C. Reilly, and Richard Jenkins. The trio then turns to praising one another's actual work — Inside Llewyn Davis as Oscar Isaac's defining performance, An Education and the long-take Maestro restaurant scene as Carey Mulligan's — before landing together on the doctrinal closing principle the panel converges on: tiny-cast contained films like Ex Machina and Midsommar produce something more epic than most sprawling blockbusters. Lee Sung Jin's three-times-in-the-theater Midsommar testimony closes the list as his stated favorite A24 film.

key_quotes.quote: Honestly, Old Boy and I did not know it was legal to make movies like that.
key_quotes.context: Lee Sung Jin's reaction to Park Chan-wook's 2003 Oldboy is the panel's clearest piece of testimony about what cinephile-defining films do to a young filmmaker's sense of what is possible. The "legal" framing is precise — Oldboy contains material that broke every rule of contemporary mainstream cinema, including the famous hammer-fight long take, the live octopus consumed on camera, and the third-act incest revelation that recontextualizes everything that came before it. For a working filmmaker,

key_quotes.quote: Handmaiden. It's the last movie I saw with my mom.
key_quotes.context: Lee Sung Jin's anecdote about watching The Handmaiden, the 2016 Park Chan-wook erotic period thriller adapted from Sarah Waters's Fingersmith, with his mother is the panel's most cinematically vivid personal moment. The Handmaiden contains several extended explicit sex scenes between its two female leads, scenes that operate at the frontier of what mainstream international cinema permitted in the mid-2010s, and Lee Sung Jin's framing at [00:32] — "I'd be looking over at mom like, 'Mom, you knew

key_quotes.quote: Is giving an Oscar-worthy performance. He's like, 'I don't care that this is a kids' film.'
key_quotes.context: Carey Mulligan's defense of Javier Bardem's title-role performance in the 2022 Lyle, Lyle, Crocodile is the panel's most argumentatively developed piece of craft criticism. Bardem, an Oscar winner for No Country for Old Men with a career of prestige drama behind him, took on the role of the singing showman Hector P. Valenti in a children's musical comedy adaptation of Bernard Waber's picture book and reportedly approached the work with the same level of preparation he brings to any of his presti

key_quotes.quote: I'm sitting next to two people who are going to come up with like cool answers, but mine are all Steven Spielberg movies.
key_quotes.context: Mulligan's self-deprecating intro to her Spielberg picks is structurally the most confident statement on the panel. The line presents itself as embarrassment about the conventionality of her taste, but the argument she immediately makes — that Jurassic Park has every beat where it should be — is a craft commitment that doesn't require defending because the films don't require defending. Most cinephile-list panels at this level of taste avoid Spielberg because admiring him in 2026 reads as basic,

key_quotes.quote: All the beats are where they should be, and it's very rewarding.
key_quotes.context: Mulligan's compressed explanation of why she rewatches Jurassic Park is the panel's most useful piece of formal-criticism vocabulary. The "beats are where they should be" framing is the language of screenwriting and of film editing — every story has structural beats that need to land at specific intervals to produce the audience's expected emotional rhythm, and a perfectly engineered film has each beat located exactly where the audience's nervous system needs it to be. Spielberg's signature, in

key_quotes.quote: I love Oscar in Inside Llewyn Davis. I think it's like one of the most incredible performances of the last 20 years.
key_quotes.context: Lee Sung Jin's ranking of Oscar Isaac's Llewyn Davis performance against every other major actor's work of the period is the panel's most specific piece of mutual-praise criticism. The 2013 Coen Brothers winter-folk-revival drama Inside Llewyn Davis follows its title character through one week of professional failure, romantic failure, and family failure, and Isaac plays Llewyn as a man whose specific gift — a beautiful folk-revival voice and a deeply felt musical sensibility — is being slowly b

key_quotes.quote: It's Ari's best work and I I watched it three times in the theater.
key_quotes.context: Lee Sung Jin's testimony about Ari Aster's 2019 Midsommar is the panel's clearest piece of repeat-viewing evidence as ranking criterion. Most film panels generate polite nominations — works the speaker has seen and admired but has not necessarily returned to. Three theatrical viewings of a single film — paying for a ticket, sitting through the full duration, repeating the process twice more — is the kind of investment a working filmmaker only makes when the film is doing something they need to k

key_quotes.quote: It's so contained and then feels so epic. It's brilliant. That one. That's my favorite.
key_quotes.context: The closing line of the entire panel and the doctrinal statement the three panelists converge on. The grammatical compression — "so contained and then feels so epic" — captures the inverse-scaling principle the whole conversation has been pointing toward. Most studio-cinema logic assumes that bigger casts, bigger sets, and bigger budgets produce bigger feelings. The panel's closing position is the opposite hypothesis: that strict containment, three characters in a glass house in Ex Machina or a

4PIGS2Olook.title: Rose Byrne & Emily Blunt | The A24 Podcast
4PIGS2Olook.synopsis: This is The A24 Podcast pairing of Rose Byrne and Emily Blunt — two performers in the middle of a 2025 awards-season run for two of the year's most discussed A24 releases. Byrne is promoting Mary Bronstein's *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You*, the second film of Bronstein's career and her first in seventeen years (her debut, *Yeast*, came out in 2008 and starred a young Greta Gerwig before Bronstein quit filmmaking to raise her family). Byrne plays Linda, a psychotherapist whose daughter has a pediatric feeding disorder, whose ceiling has collapsed into a literal black hole, and whose nervous breakdown unfolds across the film in claustrophobic 35mm close-ups, sometimes pushed inches from her eyeball. Her performance has won the Silver Bear at Berlin, an Independent Spirit Award, a Golden Globe, and an Academy Award nomination. Blunt is promoting Benny Safdie's *The Smashing Machine*, which premiered at Venice 2025 and won Safdie the Silver Lion for Best Director. Blunt plays Dawn Staples, the real-life wife of MMA pioneer Mark Kerr, opposite Dwayne Johnson, whose physical and demeanor transformation under Kazu Hiro's prosthetics has been called the performance of his career. The two actors trade detailed shop talk: the rehearsal economies of independent film, the 25-day Bronstein shoot, the matchmaking story of how Blunt connected Johnson and Safdie from the Oppenheimer makeup trailer, the emotional advocacy work of playing a real living woman who agreed to be portrayed during the most volatile period of her own life, and a long pointed conversation about why the word "likable" is a trap for women's roles and a free pass for men's. Costume designer Heidi Bivens, the choreography of physical transformation, the difference between Bronstein's in-your-face 35mm and Safdie's prowling long lens, and the question of what kind of cinema A24 is still able to make in an industry that increasingly demands "an action scene within ten minutes" all enter the conversation. The episode closes on shared affection for Australian actor Kick Gurry, Blunt's *Edge of Tomorrow* castmate.

key_quotes.quote: I wanted to scream directly into the pillow with you watching you in this very slow-paced nervous breakdown, which is one of the most painful types of nervous breakdowns to encounter that it doesn't happen all at once. It creeps in on you insidiously.
key_quotes.context: Blunt's opening articulation of what *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You* is doing functions as a piece of close criticism delivered as a compliment. She is identifying the film's core technical achievement — the slow accumulation of breakdown rather than the dramatic single event — as the source of its specific power. The "scream into the pillow" image is doing two things at once. It captures the audience's bodily response to watching Linda unravel (the urge to muffle a reaction), and it implies that B

key_quotes.quote: I like to call him Turts just to really really emasculate him.
key_quotes.context: Blunt's disclosure of her personal nickname for Dwayne Johnson is the conversation's load-bearing piece of biographical information. It establishes the easy, irreverent, on-equal-footing nature of their actual friendship — without which the matchmaker plot point arriving twenty-six minutes later would not be plausible. Johnson is the world's highest-paid male movie star, with a forty-year self-built brand of muscular invincibility called The Rock. The fact that Blunt is permitted to call him Tur

key_quotes.quote: The stress I felt watching you was rather unparalleled to anything I've seen before and the impact it had on me.
key_quotes.context: Blunt is making one of the strongest possible critical claims about *If I Had Legs I'd Kick You* — that Byrne's performance produces a viewer-side stress response that exceeds any previous comparable performance Blunt has watched. The claim arrives from someone with extraordinary professional access to other actresses' work, having co-starred with or shared sets with most of the leading dramatic performers of her generation. "Rather unparalleled" is the operative phrase. Blunt is locating Byrne'

key_quotes.quote: I'm going to advocate for you. I will take care of you. So, if you tell me the full weather system of your story, I'll make sure it's in the movie.
key_quotes.context: This is Blunt quoting back the verbatim promise she made to the real Dawn Staples in their first conversation, knowing Staples was nervous about being depicted again on screen after the John Hyams documentary had partially villainized her. The line is the cleanest articulation of an actor's ethical practice in the entire episode. Blunt is naming three commitments — advocacy, care, and full inclusion — that are not standard parts of a biographical-performance preparation. She is also using a prec

key_quotes.quote: I know that it's not for the faint of heart, and it's not everyone's love story, but it was mine.
key_quotes.context: Blunt is quoting back what the real Dawn Staples said to her early in their conversations about the marriage to Mark Kerr — the marriage being depicted in *The Smashing Machine* across the most volatile period of both their lives. The framing of the line as Staples' own self-description is doing crucial work in the film's reception. *The Smashing Machine* is being widely read as a film about codependency, addiction, and emotional violence. Staples is here insisting that it is also a love story —

key_quotes.quote: I think The Rock is the performance of a lifetime, I'm realizing now. And I said, 'How did you make that character The Rock? How did — that's a self-generated character.' And he said, 'No one's ever spoken to me about The Rock as an acting performance.'
key_quotes.context: Blunt is producing the most analytically interesting working-actor framing in the entire episode. The claim is that The Rock — the public persona Dwayne Johnson built across forty years of professional wrestling and action filmmaking — is itself an acting performance, and one that Johnson has never had previously identified as such by another working actor. The implication is a complete recontextualization of Johnson's career. His twenty-year stretch of action films are not movies of Dwayne John

key_quotes.quote: Sometimes my issue with a lot of female characters in movies is that they're sort of held in this ideal that they have to be feminine and good and palatable.
key_quotes.context: Byrne is articulating the core structural argument that runs through both films being discussed and through both actresses' professional careers. The three adjectives — feminine, good, palatable — name the historical demand that Hollywood has made of women's roles since cinema began. "Palatable" is the load-bearing word, and it is also the word that Mary Bronstein has elsewhere identified as the term that makes her "spine crumble." The line is doing the political work of identifying not just a p

key_quotes.quote: He said, 'I want it voyeuristic. I want you to feel like, oh god, I shouldn't be in the kitchen with them. This is so intimate. I shouldn't be watching this.'
key_quotes.context: Blunt is quoting Benny Safdie's directorial brief to her on set for *The Smashing Machine*. The voyeuristic framing — long lenses, prowling, hiding behind walls — is the structural opposite of Mary Bronstein's in-your-face 35mm strategy on Byrne's film. Safdie wanted the audience positioned as a guilty witness to the marriage of Mark and Dawn rather than as an empathic occupant of either of their interior lives. The "I shouldn't be in the kitchen with them" image is the precise emotional effect

key_quotes.quote: Wonderbra and fillets in there. They were like under my chin. Now you could see every man on set was like that. Like not looking, looking, not looking, looking, not looking — looking at the at your hairline.
key_quotes.context: Blunt is describing the costume process for Dawn Staples with designer Heidi Bivens, who outfitted Blunt in a Wonderbra so heavily padded with silicone fillets that, by Blunt's account, the silhouette read as comically exaggerated even on set. The "every man on set was like that" passage is the conversation's funniest piece of physical comedy delivered as costume disclosure. The specific choreography Blunt is describing — the shuffling eye-line of a male crew trying to maintain professional deco

key_quotes.quote: There's such a free pass for male characters to be unhinged. It's celebrated. I would I would I would go further. And then they're like, but you know, he really came through for his kid at the end.
key_quotes.context: Byrne is naming the precise asymmetry that her press-cycle conversations have been organized around, and her articulation is the most direct line on the subject either actress delivers. The "free pass" is the structural permission that male characters have always had to behave badly without losing audience sympathy, often with a small redemptive gesture at the end of the third act sufficing to retroactively recolor everything previous. Byrne's parenthetical example — "he really came through for

GlCaEnEAhoM.title: Rose Byrne’s Closet Picks
GlCaEnEAhoM.synopsis: Actor Rose Byrne walks the aisles of the Criterion Collection's closet and pulls down six films that taught her how cinema works: Jane Campion's The Piano, Danny Boyle's Trainspotting, David Lynch's Mulholland Dr., Wim Wenders' Paris, Texas, Mike Leigh's Secrets & Lies, and Paul Mazursky's An Unmarried Woman. The picks share a hidden through-line — every one is built around a breakout female performance, and every one uses landscape (the New Zealand coast, the Texas desert, Hollywood, Manhattan) as more than a backdrop. Byrne treats Holly Hunter, Anna Paquin, Naomi Watts, Brenda Blethyn, Marianne Jean-Baptiste, and Jill Clayburgh as her teachers. She talks about Mulholland Dr.'s sound design as the spine of the film. She remembers buying the CD of Ry Cooder's Paris, Texas score and listening to it alone in her bedroom as a teenager. She praises Mike Leigh's actors for performances where "you don't see the homework, you just see the performance." And in the final pick — An Unmarried Woman — she connects two halves of her own life: the cinephile teenager in Australia and the working actress who got to share a set with Jill Clayburgh on Bridesmaids near the end of Clayburgh's career. The interview is short but plays as a personal canon: how a working actor uses other people's performances as a continuing education.

key_quotes.quote: And the location of this place is visionary.
key_quotes.context: Byrne's first words about her first pick aren't about Holly Hunter's Oscar-winning lead or Anna Paquin's debut — they're about the New Zealand landscape that made The Piano possible. She uses the word "visionary" to describe a place rather than a person, which is unusual: most directors get the visionary label, locations rarely do. The choice reveals how Byrne reads cinema as an actor: a place that holds the camera and the performance with equal weight is itself a creative achievement. The Piano

key_quotes.quote: Danny Boyle's Trainspotting. This movie is so punk.
key_quotes.context: Byrne uses "punk" not as a genre label — Trainspotting is barely about music, despite its Iggy Pop and Lou Reed soundtrack — but as a description of filmmaking method. Punk in her usage means refusal of polish, speed over precision, anarchy as principle. Boyle's freeze-frames, hyperactive cuts, and gallows humor (the toilet sequence she names a few seconds later) translate the energy of late-1970s British punk into a 1996 film about Edinburgh heroin addicts. For a teenager watching it on first r

key_quotes.quote: The sound design in this film is so singular and so eerie.
key_quotes.context: Byrne names sound design before she names anyone in Mulholland Dr. — not Naomi Watts, not the cinematography, not the famous diner scene she'll mention thirty seconds later. That ordering is the Lynch fact most people miss. Lynch's films build their dread through low rumbles, room tone treated as score, dialogue mixed unnaturally close, and sustained sub-bass that sits below conscious hearing. The sound on Mulholland Dr. is doing the horror work that the visuals only suggest. Byrne, whose career

key_quotes.quote: an early film-education movie that changed the way that I could see cinema and the way I could see storytelling and direction
key_quotes.context: Byrne reaches into her own personal history to explain what Paris, Texas did for her, calling it her "film-education movie." That phrase is more specific than it sounds. Paris, Texas is a slow, deliberate, dialogue-light German-American road movie about a man wandering out of the desert to find his estranged son and missing wife. For a child or young teenager — Byrne says she saw it "very, very young" [02:19] — the film teaches patience as a cinematic value, time as a narrative tool, and silence

key_quotes.quote: who transformed the kitchen-sink drama into the most operatic, intense, crushing experience
key_quotes.context: "Kitchen-sink drama" is a specific British post-war tradition — gritty social realism set in working-class homes, rooted in plays and films from John Osborne, Alan Sillitoe, Tony Richardson, and Ken Loach. The form is associated with restraint, dry naturalism, and a refusal of melodrama. Byrne's claim is that Mike Leigh took that exact tradition and turned its dial in the opposite direction toward opera — emotional scale, devastating reveal, characters whose ordinary lives suddenly carry the wei

key_quotes.quote: but you don't see the homework, you just see the performance
key_quotes.context: This is the sentence of the entire interview — a working actress paying another set of actors the highest professional compliment she has. Mike Leigh famously develops his films over months of unrecorded rehearsal: actors are cast individually, build characters in isolation, then meet each other in character and improvise scenes that Leigh records, transcribes, and shapes into a script. The "homework" Byrne is referring to is that whole hidden process — the labor of building a real person in a r

key_quotes.quote: It's a little bit like I'm in a dream, so thank you so much.
key_quotes.context: Byrne closes the interview with a stock phrase — "It's a little bit like I'm in a dream" — but the line lands harder than its context suggests. She has just spent four minutes pulling six films off Criterion's shelves and naming them as the work that taught her how cinema operates. For an actress to walk through a curated archive of her medium and select what counts as essential is a strange privilege; for that actress to be told the picks will be circulated as a record of her taste is stranger

PaWwFJFhd7E.title: Spike Lee & Edward Norton | Live From Cherry Lane Theatre
PaWwFJFhd7E.synopsis: Spike Lee and Edward Norton sit onstage at New York's Cherry Lane Theatre — the 102-year-old Greenwich Village playhouse that Norton used as the rehearsal space for his own directorial debut Keeping the Faith — for a retrospective on their 2002 film 25th Hour. Norton traces the partnership back to the fan letter he wrote Spike after seeing He Got Game, and earlier to seeing Do the Right Thing in a New York theater as a young man and walking back to the box office to buy a second ticket and see it again — the film, he says, that "radicalized" his idea of what cinema could do, and the same film he and Philip Seymour Hoffman discussed for years before either of them had the chance to work with Spike. Spike walks through the decision to take David Benioff's pre-9/11 script and shoot it as the first feature film made in New York after the attacks, framed against locations directly overlooking Ground Zero where workers were still raking through rubble for human remains, scored by Terrence Blanchard. They surface a string of stories from that fall — both of them stranded in Los Angeles on September 11 trying to get home, Spike taking the last train out of LA and riding three days to New York via Chicago after a meeting with Arnold Schwarzenegger about an unproduced Joe Louis–Max Schmeling boxing film called Save Us, Joe Louis. They cover the famous mirror monologue (a passage David Benioff thought was uncinematic and not in the script — Spike said "leave that to me" and connected it directly to the spaghetti-bender insults of Do the Right Thing); Spike's Dirty Dozen approach to a 26-day shoot built on five weeks of nine-to-five rehearsal in Brooklyn and nightly screenings of Midnight Cowboy and William Wyler's Dead End to align everyone's "frequency"; the editor Barry Brown, whom Spike has worked with since 1980 in Atlanta and who cut Highest 2 Lowest; and the Hoffman-and-Pepper Chinese restaurant scene that Norton calls the truest portrait of friendship-bound-by-history he's seen on film. Lee closes with the line he gives his NYU students: do the work, do the work, do the work — and remember it's a team.

key_quotes.quote: It so radicalized my idea of what a film was that it like Phil and I used to talk about it. Kind of changed the aspirational frame we had for like what what you could do.
key_quotes.context: Norton's account of seeing Do the Right Thing as a young man in New York and walking out and immediately buying a second ticket is the foundational origin story of his whole working relationship with Spike Lee. The "aspirational frame" language is the load-bearing phrase. He is not saying the film impressed him. He is saying it changed what he thought was possible — what he and his friend Philip Seymour Hoffman thought a film could attempt. The reason this matters for understanding 25th Hour is

key_quotes.quote: We got to make this a post 9/11 film. We were the first film to shoot in New York City after that day.
key_quotes.context: The single most consequential decision in the production of 25th Hour, stated as flatly as it was apparently made. David Benioff's script had been finished before September 11. The standard industry response to receiving a strong New York–set script in the immediate aftermath of the attacks would have been to delay until the city was less raw or to scrub the New York specificity out of the material. Spike did the opposite — he amplified the city, embedded the script's grief in the actual geograp

key_quotes.quote: It's called Save Us, Joe Louis. About the relationship between Joe Louis and Max Schmeling. And I wanted Arnold to play Maxon.
key_quotes.context: One of the more cinematically vivid biographical details in Spike's body of work is the unproduced film he has been trying to make for decades about the historical 1936-1938 boxing matchups between American heavyweight champion Joe Louis and German fighter Max Schmeling, two of the most politically loaded fights of the 20th century. The detail Spike adds here is that the meeting he had set up to discuss casting Schwarzenegger as Schmeling was scheduled in Los Angeles for the morning of September

key_quotes.quote: At the time that Do the Right Thing came out, it really felt like this feeling of I can't believe that someone is saying that, I can't believe that someone's pushing in on people, saying all the inner monologues out loud.
key_quotes.context: Norton's analysis of why Do the Right Thing landed so hard in 1989 is one of the cleanest critical formulations anyone has offered of Spike's contribution to American cinema. The argument is that the racial dialogue Spike put on screen — the things characters in Bed-Stuy say to and about each other on the hottest day of summer — was at the time confined to what Norton calls a private "national consciousness," a body of language Americans were thinking but not saying aloud. Spike's move was to ex

key_quotes.quote: And Spike goes, 'Leave that to me.'
key_quotes.context: The three-word answer that resulted in the most-discussed sequence in 25th Hour. David Benioff, the screenwriter and novelist, had cut the diet-trap passage from his own adaptation because he judged it to be uncinematic interior monologue. Norton found the passage in the novel, asked Spike where it had gone in the script, and Spike said "leave that to me." The exchange captures something specific about Spike's working authority on a film. Benioff's caution was professionally responsible — uncine

key_quotes.quote: I always feel that you in the end love everybody even the racist pizza maker.
key_quotes.context: Norton's most generous reading of Spike's body of work is that the camera, even when it is looking at characters whose behavior is racist or violent or cruel, is always operating in a register of compassion rather than condemnation. The Sal example is well-chosen — the pizzeria owner in Do the Right Thing is the character through whose window Mookie throws the garbage can, and the film does not soften his racism, but it also does not let him become only his racism. The argument matters because m

key_quotes.quote: The 25th Hour we made in 26 days. The budget and the schedule was the shortest that I'd ever done on.
key_quotes.context: The economic clarification that makes everything else in the production make sense. 25th Hour is a film featuring Norton, Philip Seymour Hoffman, Barry Pepper, Rosario Dawson, Brian Cox, and Anna Paquin, shot in New York City with location access to Ground Zero, scored by Terrence Blanchard, photographed by Rodrigo Prieto, and cut by Barry Brown. By any reasonable accounting, that is a major production. It was made in 26 days. The line lands inside Norton's running argument with Spike about whet

key_quotes.quote: As soon as it ended, Spike Spike goes, 'Still a motherfucker.' I remember that.
key_quotes.context: Norton's account of Spike's three-word review of John Schlesinger's Midnight Cowboy after a cast screening during the 25th Hour rehearsal block is one of the most specific portraits of what film authority sounds like in working conditions. Spike had selected Midnight Cowboy specifically — he and cinematographer Rodrigo Prieto wanted to study its visual approach and use it as inspiration. The cast had not seen the 1969 film on a screen in some time. They watched it together. When the final credit

key_quotes.quote: I've been gifted to having great people like you in front of, you know, in front of the camera and behind the camera. So it's a team. It's a team.
key_quotes.context: The clearest articulation Spike gives in the interview of his working philosophy. The team-and-win frame is a sports analogy applied to filmmaking, which is an industry that does not always accept it. Most film directors are mythologized as solo authors imposing a vision. Spike is publicly insisting that the actual unit of production is the team and that long careers are built by long collaborations. The example he is sitting next to onstage is Norton, the actor who wrote him a fan letter as a y

key_quotes.quote: They really leaned into the the the truth of how much love hate can be in a in a friendship that's bound up in history as opposed to present connection.
key_quotes.context: Norton's closing critical observation on the Hoffman-and-Pepper Chinese restaurant scene as the most truthful piece of friendship portraiture in 25th Hour, and possibly in his career as a viewer. The argument is structural rather than emotional. Most film drama cleans up long friendships into mutual liking because mutual liking is the more legible audience-facing register. Real long friendships are more complicated — they are bound by accumulated history, and the history contains contempt as wel

_9xC6n-gyQI.title: The Brutalist | Behind the Score with Composer Daniel Blumberg
_9xC6n-gyQI.synopsis: Daniel Blumberg, the London musician who came up in Cajun Dance Party and Yuck before going solo on Mute and won the Oscar for Best Original Score for Brady Corbet's The Brutalist, walks through the working philosophy behind the score in three minutes flat. Everything was meant to feel live. Tubas, trombones, and trumpets played the industrial-emergency sirens that opened the sound mix. The avant-garde pianist John Tilbury, brought in by Blumberg, performed a prepared piano session — screws and hardware shoved into the piano strings until the instrument sounded like hammers hitting material — because the film is about construction and the piano needed to be construction. Brady Corbet shot certain scenes to the score — the Overture was blasted through speakers as Adrien Brody, playing the Hungarian-Jewish architect László Tóth arriving from the camps in Europe to America, moved to the music in real time, and the cinematographer danced the camera along with it. Other scenes had live music played on set, including a jazz sequence performed by a band Blumberg flew in from Paris, Marseille, and Berlin. For the section of the film set in the Carrara marble quarries in Italy, Blumberg drove there himself to record the actual valley's reverb — they fired a gunshot, captured the echo as an impulse response, and then digitally pushed Evan Parker's soprano saxophone through that quarry-shaped reverberation, so the music in the Carrara sequences is physically vibrating through the same geography being filmed onscreen. Blumberg closes with the working tension: the score had to retain its live, breathing quality while still hitting the precise edit beats — and when it works, he says, it produces something that feels alive.

key_quotes.quote: We wanted the performances to be live like something was happening.
key_quotes.context: The single doctrinal sentence that organizes every other technical decision in the score for The Brutalist. Live-ness is not a stylistic preference for Daniel Blumberg — it is the structural principle that distinguishes his score from conventional Hollywood orchestration, in which performance precision and repeatable takes are prized over the documentary feel of a single irreversible event. The phrase "like something was happening" captures the deeper claim. The score is not commenting on the wo

key_quotes.quote: For a film about construction I did a session on the prepared piano. The piano is made up of many hammers that hammer the strings, and if you interfere with the strings by putting screws and objects into the strings you can get these strange percussive sounds.
key_quotes.context: Blumberg's compressed explanation of the prepared piano session with John Tilbury contains the entire working logic of his approach to scoring The Brutalist. The piano is not selected for its melodic register but for its underlying mechanism — it is fundamentally a hammer-and-string instrument, which is also fundamentally a description of construction. The interference with the strings turns the instrument from a producer of harmonic music into a producer of "percussive sounds," which is the son

key_quotes.quote: This scene where Adrian's character László is coming essentially from the Holocaust from the camps into America, it was being blasted out on the speakers.
key_quotes.context: Blumberg's description of the Overture playing live on the set as Adrien Brody filmed László Tóth's arrival in America is one of the more specific portraits of how a film score can become an active participant in production rather than a postproduction overlay. The narrative weight of the scene is enormous — it is the beginning of the protagonist's American life after surviving the Nazi camps in Europe — and Brady Corbet's decision to shoot it to the music meant that the rhythm of Brody's perfor

key_quotes.quote: All the actors could move to the music and the cinematographer could dance to the music with the camera.
key_quotes.context: Blumberg's description of the jazz scene — for which he flew a band of specific improvisers from Paris, Marseille, and Berlin to perform live on set — captures something that mainstream film production almost never admits in public. Camera operation, especially in long takes and complex blocking, is itself a form of dance — a physical performance with timing, weight, and rhythm that is normally improvised against the actors and the choreography. 6yQ-i55eeGY.title: Tyler Okonma | A24 x Letterboxd: The List
6yQ-i55eeGY.synopsis: Tyler Okonma — Tyler, the Creator — appears on the A24 x Letterboxd "The List" series in late 2025 / early 2026 in the immediate wake of his feature film debut in Josh Safdie's *Marty Supreme*, the December 25, 2025 A24 release in which he plays Wally opposite Timothée Chalamet. *Marty Supreme* became A24's second-biggest opening week ever and the most expensive film in the studio's history at a $60-70M budget. Tyler's casting was written for him by Safdie after the two became friends following a 2017 Tyler show, and Tyler shot the role the same week his Grammy-nominated 2024 album *CHROMAKOPIA* was released. Knowing this is the reason he is on the show clarifies why A24 gave him the open-format Letterboxd top-four prompt and why he is rotating through the kind of cinephile disclosure usually reserved for working directors. The episode runs only 6:46 but is unusually dense. Tyler opens with *Napoleon Dynamite* — the first DVD his mother bought him, watched on his sister's Hello Kitty television, soundtracked by Jamiroquai's "Canned Heat," and revisited seven times in a row on tour until his Odd Future collaborator Jasper Dolphin begged him to turn it off. He moves through Chris Robinson's *ATL* (the Aaliyah-scored car scene with T.I. and Lauren London hits a frequency he recognizes from his own childhood), Bo Welch's *The Cat in the Hat* (which he champions despite a Hollywood producer once telling him face-to-face that the film "ruined everything"), Brian Robbins' *Good Burger* ("the Bible" of his humor), and Rick Famuyiwa's *The Wood* — which Tyler reveals was filmed in the actual cafeteria at La Tijera Elementary School in Inglewood while Tyler was a third-grader there. He continues into the Wayans family's *Don't Be a Menace*, *Scary Movie 2*, and *I'm Gonna Git You Sucka*; Wayne Kramer's *Running Scared* (compared to Good Time's escalating weirdness); F. Gary Gray's *The Italian Job*; and Danny DeVito's *Matilda* (a piece of color-theory analysis on the drained school palette and Miss Honey's flower dresses). The closing minutes are a rapid-fire DVD inventory — physical media as Tyler's preferred mode of cinematic study — leading into a *Pootie Tang* endorsement, a *Y Tu Mamá También* envy moment, and a Michael Haneke *Funny Games* sign-off.

The Hello Kitty television with the built-in DVD player is the kind of mid-2000s branded children's product that briefly defined a particular middle-class consumer aesthetic. key_quotes.context: Tyler's seven-consecutive-viewings-on-tour disclosure is the moment the *Napoleon Dynamite* obsession crosses from childhood nostalgia into working-artist research. Watching a single film seven times in a row inside the confined space of a tour bus is structurally what working directors do with reference material when they are trying to absorb a particular formal solution. key_quotes.quote: I met with some movie producer right and he was telling me all these movies and all these great things he did and I was like dude Cat in the Hat and he was like oh my god that movie ruined everything.

The unnamed producer walked him through an impressive professional history. Tyler frames his birth-year proximity to the film's release as "lucky," which is a precise piece of generational self-location — he was born in 1991, the film came out in 1997, and his comedic absorption period coincided with the late-'90s Nickelodeon era. key_quotes.quote: Don't be a menace. They really dialed in into their shape of satire, which I think was the real bed, the the the real flower bed for the bloom that Scary Movie 2 was.
