MQZuHbd-rTw.title: Vince Staples’s Closet Picks
MQZuHbd-rTw.synopsis: Vince Staples enters the Criterion Closet and opens with a tribute he says he wishes more people made out loud — a long thank-you to Robert Townsend for *Hollywood Shuffle*, *The Five Heartbeats*, and *The Meteor Man*. He turns the visit into a public plea for someone to send him a copy of *The Five Heartbeats* on any format. He picks David Cronenberg's *Videodrome* as a movie that has stopped being science fiction because we are now actually living inside its premise about humans imitating the media they consume. He selects Alejandro Amenábar's *The Others* as a hit, frankly admitting he likes star power and is here for Nicole Kidman in a movie that scared him as a kid. He grabs Andrew Stanton's *WALL·E* off the shelf and notes, with affection, that animation has always mattered to him. He picks the Criterion Eclipse box set of Jean-Pierre Gorin's three Southern California documentaries and singles out *My Crasy Life*, the 1991 film about the Sons of Samoa community in West Long Beach, because Staples grew up knowing people with family roots in that exact community and considers the movie's refusal of forced happy endings to be the only honest way to chronicle his neighborhood. He chooses Richard Pryor's *Jo Jo Dancer, Your Life Is Calling* — the comedian's only film as a director — because the worldview it carries matches the cynical-but-hopeful nihilism Staples grew up around in Long Beach. He pulls down the Melvin Van Peebles Essential Films set and uses *Watermelon Man* and *Sweet Sweetback's Baadasssss Song* to argue that Van Peebles is the founding father whose first studio film created the leverage for the second, which created the entire blaxploitation genre — *Coffy*, *Shaft*, *I'm Gonna Git You Sucka*. He closes with *Lynch/Oz*, the Alexandre O. Philippe documentary about David Lynch's lifelong obsession with *The Wizard of Oz*, because Staples has been stealing from Oz at every opportunity in his own work and watching the doc gave him permission to admit that even Lynch's darkest experiments rest on the most classical reference point in American cinema.

key_quotes.quote: Robert Townsend, you do not get enough credit. We love you for your creativity.
key_quotes.context: The opening tribute that sets the loyalty tone of the whole visit. Staples turns the camera into a public-address system and uses it to register a debt that Hollywood has been unwilling to pay — Townsend financed *Hollywood Shuffle* on credit cards, made *The Five Heartbeats* against an industry that did not want a 1960s R&B biopic, and then largely vanished from the conversation. Staples's first sentence is the correction.

key_quotes.quote: Do me a favor. If anyone has a DVD of The Five Heartbeats, Blu-ray — it doesn't have to be Blu-ray — anything you have, send that to me.
key_quotes.context: The most genuinely funny piece of advocacy in the visit. Staples uses his own platform to publicly request a copy of a 1991 musical biopic that has been so chronically out of circulation that even a famous artist with industry connections cannot find one. The line doubles as a piece of physical-media commentary — the things we love do not stay available unless someone keeps the discs alive.

key_quotes.quote: You're on the internet. You're on the television. We're imitating what we see through our art, through our lives, the way that we communicate.
key_quotes.context: Staples translating Cronenberg's 1983 *Videodrome* into 2026 terms. The line is the cleanest contemporary read of what the film was actually predicting — not body horror as metaphor but media saturation as a literal rewriting of how humans behave. Staples's framing is the point: what was once a midnight-movie premise is now a Tuesday afternoon.

key_quotes.quote: Certified banger. Watch The Others.
key_quotes.context: The visit's most efficient endorsement. Staples has just finished a forty-second monologue about loving Meryl Streep, Julia Roberts, and Viola Davis, and his closing assessment of Amenábar's 2001 film — a Nicole Kidman ghost story that scared him as a kid — is two sentences. The brevity is the point: not every pick needs an academic defense, and a "certified banger" recommendation from a serious artist is its own form of criticism.

key_quotes.quote: Tell our stories: they don't have to be big, they don't have to be great. We don't need happy or bad endings. Let's just chronicle life as we know it because it's ever so changing.
key_quotes.context: The clearest articulation of Staples's artistic position in the entire visit. The line is delivered while holding Gorin's *My Crasy Life* and is the philosophical engine behind *The Vince Staples Show*, *Ramona Park Broke My Heart*, and *Dark Times*. Staples is making a case against the Hollywood demand for redemption arcs and against the documentary tradition's demand for sociological resolution, in favor of work that simply records.

key_quotes.quote: You take something and try to create something holistic that will be free of regret, that'll be free of fear, that'll be free of just anxiety.
key_quotes.context: Staples's distillation of what he learned from Melvin Van Peebles. The line is delivered while explaining how *Watermelon Man* leveraged Van Peebles into the position of being able to make *Sweet Sweetback's Baadasssss Song* on his own terms. The triplet — regret, fear, anxiety — is the working artist's actual list of obstacles, and Staples is naming Van Peebles as the model for working past all three.

key_quotes.quote: I too steal from The Wizard of Oz at every chance I can.
key_quotes.context: The confession that opens the closing pick. Staples uses the word "too" because he has just been watching the *Lynch/Oz* documentary, which spends 108 minutes proving that David Lynch has been doing the same thing for fifty years. The line is also the visit's most direct statement about Staples's actual creative method — that the dark, surreal, and difficult work he is best known for is built on top of one of the most familiar pieces of classical American imagery anyone could choose.

key_quotes.quote: Not that The Wizard of Oz was pure, because justice for Judy Garland and we will never forget you, and if we find out who did that to Judy, we'll dig you up.
key_quotes.context: One of the most extraordinary closing runs in any Criterion Closet visit. Staples acknowledges the genuine darkness of the actual production history of *The Wizard of Oz* — the documented mistreatment of Judy Garland on set, the diet pills, the working conditions — and pivots to a half-joking promise of retribution. The line captures everything the visit has been arguing: that even pure classical imagery has trauma underneath it, and that Black artists like Staples treat that contradiction as th
